title: Management Quiz
description: Patient Rights, Ethics, Care Coordination, Legal Regulations, Leadership

questions.text: A nurse is creating a care plan for a patient and prioritizes addressing the patient's acute pain over their need for nutritional education. This prioritization is primarily based on which framework?
options: The facility's clinical pathway for the diagnosis.
questions.explanation: Consider a well-known psychological theory that ranks human needs in order of importance.

questions.text: A patient with decision-making capacity informs the nurse that they wish to refuse a recommended surgery. This decision is protected under which ethical principle?
questions.explanation: This ethical principle emphasizes a person's right to self-governance and making their own choices.

questions.text: During a handoff report, a nurse states, 'The patient's blood pressure has been trending downward, and I am concerned about potential hypovolemia.' Which part of the SBAR technique does this statement represent?
questions.explanation: This part of the communication tool involves the caregiver's analysis of the situation.

questions.text: A hospital must decide which of two equally needy patients receives a single available organ for transplant. This scenario most directly involves the ethical principle of:
questions.explanation: Consider the ethical principle concerned with fairness and allocation of resources.

questions.text: A nurse manager who makes decisions independently and strictly enforces rules, leaving team members with little input, is demonstrating which leadership style?
questions.explanation: This leadership style is often compared to that of a military commander who expects orders to be followed.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered one of the 'Five Rights of Delegation' in nursing?
questions.explanation: This principle of delegation focuses on ensuring the individual receiving the task is competent and qualified.

questions.text: A patient recovering from a stroke no longer requires hospitalization but is not yet able to perform daily activities independently at home. Which level of care is most appropriate for this patient?
questions.explanation: This level of care serves as a bridge between the intensive environment of a hospital and returning home.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)?
options: To set minimum safety standards in the workplace.
To regulate the standards and quality of care in nursing homes.
To protect the privacy of an individual's healthcare information.
To ensure emergency treatment is provided regardless of ability to pay.
questions.explanation: This federal law is primarily associated with patient confidentiality.

questions.text: A grateful patient's family offers a nurse a valuable piece of jewelry. According to professional boundaries, what is the most appropriate action?
options: Politely decline, explaining that the care provided is part of the professional role.
Suggest they make a donation to the hospital foundation instead.
Accept the gift on behalf of the entire unit to be shared later.
Accept the gift to avoid offending the family.
questions.explanation: Consider the need to maintain a professional relationship and avoid any appearance of obligation.

questions.text: The care delivery model where an interdisciplinary team, including nurses, therapists, and social workers, collaboratively provides care to a group of patients is known as:
questions.explanation: This model emphasizes teamwork and coordination across different healthcare professions for a group of patients.

questions.text: Which federal law requires hospitals to provide medical screening and stabilization to any patient presenting to an emergency department, regardless of their ability to pay?
options: EMTALA (Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act)
questions.explanation: This law is often referred to as the anti-dumping law for emergency departments.

questions.text: In the context of research, what is the purpose of a control group?
options: To conduct a survey of the literature before the study begins.
To receive the treatment or intervention being evaluated.
To serve as a baseline for comparison by not receiving the treatment.
To ensure the sample size is large enough for statistical significance.
questions.explanation: This group provides a point of reference to measure the effect of the intervention.

questions.text: A nurse who administers a fatal dose of medication due to a calculation error has committed which type of tort?
questions.explanation: This legal term applies when harm is caused by a failure to act with reasonable care, not by a deliberate act to cause harm.

questions.text: A 70-year-old individual needs coverage for prescription drugs. Which part of Medicare should they enroll in?
questions.explanation: Think about the letter of the Medicare program specifically designed for pharmaceuticals.

questions.text: A hospital uses a system to classify patients based on their diagnosis for the purpose of determining how much the hospital should be reimbursed by Medicare. This system is known as:
questions.explanation: This billing system groups patients who share similar diagnoses and treatments.

questions.text: The process of collecting patient data through observation, interviews, and physical examination occurs during which step of the nursing process?
questions.explanation: This is the first and foundational step in the systematic approach to patient care.

questions.text: A nurse is working with a patient and their family to incorporate their cultural and spiritual beliefs into the plan of care. This action primarily addresses which factor affecting care planning?
questions.explanation: This aspect of care planning recognizes that a patient's background and belief systems significantly influence their health experiences.

questions.text: A nurse calls a physician to report a change in a patient's condition. The physician gives a new order over the phone. What is a critical step the nurse must take to ensure patient safety?
options: Tell the physician to enter the order in the computer immediately.
Implement the order immediately to ensure timely care.
Ask a colleague to listen to the order simultaneously.
Write down the order, then read it back to the physician for confirmation.
questions.explanation: This safety procedure is designed to close the communication loop and prevent errors from misinterpretation.

questions.text: What is the main goal of conflict resolution in a healthcare team setting?
options: To temporarily suppress the disagreement to maintain a quiet work environment.
To ensure the leader's initial opinion is adopted by the team.
To identify which team member was at fault.
To produce a positive outcome by opening dialogue and seeking a solution.
questions.explanation: The objective is not to win an argument, but to improve teamwork and care delivery.

questions.text: A case manager is coordinating a patient's discharge. This role primarily involves which activity?
options: Diagnosing the patient's primary medical condition.
Performing direct, hands-on nursing tasks like wound care.
Coordinating services and resources to fulfill the patient's healthcare needs.
Prescribing medications needed for the patient after discharge.
questions.explanation: This professional acts as a central point of contact to ensure a smooth transition of care.

questions.text: The use of telehealth has expanded to provide care to patients remotely. Which of the following is a primary benefit of telehealth?
options: It guarantees complete privacy with no risk of data breaches.
It lowers the overall cost of healthcare and increases patient access.
It allows for hands-on physical assessments and procedures.
It is most effective for managing acute, life-threatening emergencies.
questions.explanation: Consider the advantages of providing healthcare services without the patient needing to travel.

questions.text: Which type of research study is considered the 'gold standard' for testing the efficacy of a new treatment due to its design that minimizes bias?
questions.explanation: This study design involves randomly assigning participants to different groups to compare outcomes.

questions.text: A nurse is reviewing a patient's medical record. Which document would provide a chronological account of the patient's care, condition, and response to treatment?
questions.explanation: These entries are made regularly by doctors, nurses, and therapists to document the patient's journey.

questions.text: In evidence-based practice, what is the highest level of evidence?
options: Evidence supported by suggestive clinical studies with a theoretical basis.
Evidence from descriptive or comparative studies.
Evidence from well-designed experimental studies like RCTs.
questions.explanation: This level of evidence comes from the most rigorous type of scientific study designed to eliminate bias.

questions.text: A nurse notices that several patients have developed pressure ulcers after being placed on a new type of mattress. The first step the nurse should take as part of the quality improvement process is to:
options: Implement a new wound care protocol immediately.
Collect data on all patients with pressure ulcers in the hospital.
Identify the situation as a potential problem that could benefit from change.
questions.explanation: Think about the very first action one must take before attempting to solve any problem.

title: Management Quiz - Part B
description: Advanced Management: Delegation, Prioritization, Leadership, Quality Improvement, Legal/Ethical Issues

questions.text: A nurse manager is implementing a new electronic health record system. Which change management strategy is most effective?
options: Involve staff in planning and provide comprehensive training
Implement changes gradually without staff input
Wait for staff to request the change
questions.explanation: Effective change management involves staff participation, adequate training, and support. This increases buy-in and reduces resistance to change.

questions.text: Which task can an RN safely delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?
options: Initial patient assessment for a new admission
Administering oral medications to stable patients
Teaching a diabetic patient about insulin administration
questions.explanation: LPNs can administer medications to stable patients. Initial assessments, care planning, and patient teaching require RN-level judgment and cannot be delegated.

questions.text: A nurse receives reports on four patients. Which patient should be assessed first?
options: Patient with diabetes requesting a snack
Patient post-op day 2 with pain rated 6/10
Patient with chest pain and shortness of breath
Patient requesting assistance to the bathroom
questions.explanation: Chest pain and shortness of breath indicate potential cardiac or respiratory emergency requiring immediate assessment using the ABC priority framework.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of root cause analysis (RCA) in healthcare?
options: To assign blame for medical errors
To identify system failures that led to an adverse event
To discipline staff members involved in incidents
questions.explanation: RCA is a systematic process to identify underlying system failures, not to blame individuals. The goal is to prevent future occurrences through system improvements.

questions.text: A nurse witnesses a colleague diverting narcotics. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Report the incident to the nurse manager immediately
Wait to see if it happens again
questions.explanation: Narcotic diversion is illegal and endangers patients. The nurse has a legal and ethical duty to report immediately to protect patient safety and comply with regulations.

questions.text: Which leadership style is most effective during an emergency situation?
questions.explanation: In emergencies, autocratic leadership is most effective because it allows for rapid decision-making without delay from consensus-building.

questions.text: A patient's family requests that the patient not be told about a terminal diagnosis. How should the nurse respond?
options: Agree to withhold information to respect family wishes
Inform the patient immediately despite family wishes
Discuss with the healthcare team and respect patient autonomy
Document the request and take no action
questions.explanation: Patient autonomy takes precedence. The healthcare team should discuss the situation, but ultimately the competent patient has the right to know their diagnosis.

questions.text: Which action demonstrates transformational leadership?
options: Inspiring staff to exceed expectations through vision and motivation
Allowing staff complete freedom in decision-making
questions.explanation: Transformational leaders inspire and motivate staff to achieve beyond expectations by creating a shared vision and fostering innovation.

questions.text: A nurse manager notices increased medication errors on the unit. What is the first step in quality improvement?
options: Collect data to identify patterns and root causes
questions.explanation: Quality improvement begins with data collection and analysis to identify patterns, root causes, and contributing factors before implementing solutions.

questions.text: Which task is appropriate to delegate to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
options: Measuring and recording vital signs on stable patients
questions.explanation: UAP can perform routine tasks like vital signs on stable patients. Assessment, medication administration, and care planning require licensed personnel.

questions.text: A nurse discovers a medication error was made. What is the priority action?
options: Assess the patient for adverse effects
questions.explanation: Patient safety is the priority. First assess the patient for any adverse effects, then notify the provider, complete incident report, and document.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a variance report in case management?
options: To track deviations from the expected clinical pathway
questions.explanation: Variance reports document when a patient's progress deviates from the expected clinical pathway, helping identify issues and improve care coordination.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for four patients. Using Maslow's Hierarchy, which need should be addressed first?
questions.explanation: Physiological needs (oxygen) are the foundation of Maslow's hierarchy and must be met before higher-level needs like spiritual or social needs.

questions.text: Which situation represents a breach of patient confidentiality?
options: Discussing patient care with the healthcare team during rounds
Sharing patient information with family after obtaining consent
Discussing patient details in the hospital elevator
Documenting patient information in the medical record
questions.explanation: Discussing patient information in public areas (elevators, cafeterias) where unauthorized persons can overhear violates HIPAA and patient confidentiality.

questions.text: A nurse manager is conducting a performance evaluation. Which approach is most effective?
options: Focus only on negative behaviors that need improvement
Provide specific examples of both strengths and areas for growth
Compare the employee to other staff members
Avoid discussing weaknesses to maintain morale
questions.explanation: Effective evaluations are balanced, specific, and constructive. They acknowledge strengths while addressing areas for improvement with concrete examples.

questions.text: What is the primary goal of continuous quality improvement (CQI)?
options: To continuously enhance patient care and outcomes
questions.explanation: CQI focuses on ongoing efforts to improve patient care quality, safety, and outcomes through systematic evaluation and improvement of processes.

questions.text: A nurse is assigned to mentor a new graduate nurse. Which action best demonstrates effective mentorship?
options: Completing tasks for the new nurse to save time
Providing guidance while allowing independent practice with supervision
Criticizing mistakes publicly to ensure learning
questions.explanation: Effective mentorship balances guidance with autonomy, allowing the mentee to develop skills while providing support and constructive feedback.

questions.text: Which ethical principle is violated when a nurse shares patient information on social media?
questions.explanation: Fidelity involves maintaining trust and confidentiality. Sharing patient information on social media breaches this trust and violates HIPAA.

questions.text: A nurse manager is implementing evidence-based practice changes. What is the first step?
options: Conduct a literature review to identify best practices
questions.explanation: Evidence-based practice begins with reviewing current research and literature to identify best practices supported by scientific evidence.

questions.text: Which situation requires the nurse to act as a patient advocate?
options: A patient refuses a recommended treatment
A physician orders a treatment the patient doesn't understand
A patient asks about visiting hours
questions.explanation: Advocacy involves ensuring patients understand their care and can make informed decisions. The nurse should clarify the treatment with the physician and patient.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the Patient Self-Determination Act (PSDA)?
options: To ensure patients receive emergency treatment
To inform patients about advance directives and their right to make healthcare decisions
questions.explanation: PSDA requires healthcare facilities to inform patients about advance directives and their right to make decisions about their healthcare.

questions.text: A nurse observes unsafe practice by a physician. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Report to the nurse manager and follow chain of command
Document and take no further action
questions.explanation: Patient safety is paramount. The nurse should report unsafe practices through proper channels (nurse manager, risk management) to protect patients.

questions.text: Which action demonstrates effective conflict resolution?
options: Avoiding the conflict until it resolves itself
Listening actively to all parties and seeking mutually acceptable solutions
Taking sides based on personal relationships
questions.explanation: Effective conflict resolution involves active listening, understanding all perspectives, and collaboratively finding solutions that address everyone's concerns.

questions.text: A nurse manager is developing a staffing schedule. Which factor is most important?
questions.explanation: Patient acuity (severity of illness) and census (number of patients) are primary factors in safe staffing. While other factors matter, patient safety is paramount.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a sentinel event review?
options: To investigate unexpected events resulting in death or serious harm
questions.explanation: Sentinel events are unexpected occurrences involving death or serious harm. Reviews identify root causes and prevent recurrence.

questions.text: Which task requires an RN and cannot be delegated to an LPN?
questions.explanation: IV push medications require RN-level assessment and judgment due to rapid onset and potential for adverse reactions. LPNs cannot administer IV push meds.

questions.text: A nurse is prioritizing care for multiple patients. Which patient should be seen first?
options: Patient scheduled for discharge in 2 hours
Patient with new-onset confusion and agitation
questions.explanation: New-onset confusion may indicate serious complications (hypoxia, infection, stroke) and requires immediate assessment to identify the cause.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of interdisciplinary care rounds?
options: To improve communication and coordination among healthcare team members
To decrease length of hospital stay only
questions.explanation: Interdisciplinary rounds bring together all team members (nurses, physicians, therapists, social workers) to improve communication, coordination, and patient outcomes.

questions.text: A nurse manager notices increased staff turnover. What is the best initial approach?
options: Conduct exit interviews and staff surveys to identify causes
questions.explanation: Understanding the root causes of turnover through exit interviews and surveys allows targeted interventions to address specific issues.

questions.text: Which action violates professional boundaries?
options: Accepting a thank-you card from a patient
Providing emotional support during difficult times
Sharing personal contact information with a patient
questions.explanation: Sharing personal contact information blurs professional boundaries and can lead to inappropriate relationships. Nurses should maintain professional distance.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle in quality improvement?
options: To test and implement changes in a systematic way
questions.explanation: PDSA is a systematic method for testing changes: Plan the change, Do (implement), Study (evaluate results), Act (adopt or modify).

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient who refuses life-saving treatment due to religious beliefs. What should the nurse do?
options: Convince the patient to accept treatment
Respect the patient's decision and provide supportive care
Obtain a court order for treatment
Ignore the refusal and provide treatment anyway
questions.explanation: Competent adults have the right to refuse treatment, even life-saving treatment. The nurse must respect autonomy while providing supportive care.

questions.text: Which situation represents appropriate use of restraints?
options: Patient is pulling at IV lines despite redirection and alternatives have failed
Staffing is inadequate to monitor the patient
questions.explanation: Restraints are a last resort when patients pose immediate danger to themselves/others and all alternatives have failed. They require a physician's order.

questions.text: A nurse manager is implementing a new policy. Which communication method is most effective?
options: Staff meeting with discussion and written materials
Posting a notice on the bulletin board
questions.explanation: Effective communication uses multiple methods: verbal explanation with opportunity for questions, plus written materials for reference.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of peer review in nursing?
options: To evaluate and improve nursing practice through colleague feedback
questions.explanation: Peer review is a professional accountability mechanism where nurses evaluate each other's practice to maintain standards and promote improvement.

questions.text: A nurse is assigned to float to an unfamiliar unit. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Accept assignment and request orientation to unit-specific procedures
Call in sick to avoid floating
questions.explanation: Nurses can be required to float but should request orientation to unfamiliar equipment/procedures and only accept assignments within their competency.

questions.text: Which action demonstrates shared governance in nursing?
options: Managers make all decisions without staff input
Staff nurses participate in decision-making about practice and policies
questions.explanation: Shared governance empowers staff nurses to participate in decisions affecting their practice, promoting autonomy and professional accountability.

questions.text: A patient requests a copy of their medical record. What should the nurse do?
options: Provide information about the facility's process for obtaining records
Give the patient the chart immediately
Tell the patient only physicians can release records
questions.explanation: Under HIPAA, patients have the right to access their records. The nurse should explain the facility's formal process for requesting copies.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of clinical pathways?
options: To standardize care and improve outcomes for specific diagnoses
To eliminate the need for physician orders
questions.explanation: Clinical pathways provide evidence-based, standardized care plans for specific diagnoses to improve quality, reduce variation, and enhance outcomes.

questions.text: A nurse witnesses a colleague making a serious error. What is the ethical obligation?
options: Ignore it to maintain collegial relationships
Report the error to protect patient safety
Confront the colleague privately and take no further action
questions.explanation: Patient safety is the highest priority. The nurse has an ethical and legal duty to report errors through proper channels to prevent harm.

questions.text: Which leadership behavior promotes a positive work environment?
options: Criticizing staff in front of others
questions.explanation: Recognition and rewards promote job satisfaction, motivation, and retention. Positive reinforcement is more effective than punishment in creating a healthy work environment.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient from a different culture. What is the best approach?
options: Assume all members of that culture have the same beliefs
Ask the patient about their cultural preferences and beliefs
Ignore cultural differences and provide standard care
questions.explanation: Cultural competence requires asking patients about their individual beliefs and preferences rather than making assumptions based on stereotypes.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA)?
options: To identify potential failures before they occur
To investigate errors after they happen
questions.explanation: FMEA is a proactive risk assessment tool that identifies potential failures in processes before they occur, allowing preventive measures.

questions.text: A nurse manager is addressing chronic absenteeism. What is the most effective approach?
options: Meet privately with the employee to discuss the issue and identify solutions
questions.explanation: Progressive discipline begins with private discussion to understand causes and develop solutions. Immediate termination is rarely appropriate for first offenses.

questions.text: Which action demonstrates professional accountability?
options: Taking responsibility for one's own actions and decisions
questions.explanation: Professional accountability means taking responsibility for one's actions, decisions, and practice while maintaining professional standards.

questions.text: A nurse discovers a medication was administered to the wrong patient. What is the priority?
options: Assess both patients for adverse effects
Hide the error to avoid consequences
questions.explanation: Patient safety is paramount. Assess both patients (one who received wrong med, one who didn't receive ordered med), then notify provider and complete incident report.

questions.text: What is the primary goal of benchmarking in healthcare?
options: To compare performance with best practices and identify improvement opportunities
questions.explanation: Benchmarking compares an organization's performance against industry best practices to identify gaps and opportunities for improvement.

questions.text: A nurse is asked to perform a procedure they are not trained to do. What should the nurse do?
options: Refuse and request appropriate training or reassignment
Ask a colleague to show them once, then perform it
Look up the procedure online and attempt it
questions.explanation: Nurses must practice within their scope of competence. Performing procedures without proper training endangers patients and violates professional standards.

questions.text: Which situation represents appropriate use of social media by nurses?
options: Discussing specific patient cases without names
questions.explanation: Nurses can share general health education on social media. Any patient information, even without names, can violate HIPAA if the patient is identifiable.

questions.text: A nurse manager is implementing a new staffing model. What is the most important consideration?
options: Patient safety and quality of care
questions.explanation: While cost and preferences matter, patient safety and quality of care must be the primary consideration in any staffing decision.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a just culture in healthcare?
options: To balance accountability with learning from errors without blame
To eliminate all consequences for mistakes
To protect the organization from liability
questions.explanation: Just culture distinguishes between human error (learning opportunity), at-risk behavior (coaching), and reckless behavior (discipline), promoting safety reporting.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient who is a Jehovah's Witness refusing blood transfusion. The patient is hemorrhaging. What should the nurse do?
options: Administer blood anyway to save the patient's life
Respect the patient's wishes and explore alternative treatments
Obtain a court order for transfusion
Convince the family to override the patient's decision
questions.explanation: Competent adults have the right to refuse treatment based on religious beliefs. The nurse must respect autonomy while exploring alternatives (volume expanders, medications).

questions.text: Which action demonstrates effective time management for a nurse?
options: Completing all tasks for one patient before moving to the next
Prioritizing tasks based on patient acuity and urgency
Responding to requests in the order received
questions.explanation: Effective time management requires prioritizing based on patient needs and acuity, not simply completing tasks in order or for one patient at a time.

questions.text: A nurse manager is conducting a performance improvement project. What is the first step?
options: Identify the problem and collect baseline data
questions.explanation: Performance improvement begins with clearly identifying the problem and collecting baseline data to measure current performance before implementing changes.

questions.text: Which situation requires the nurse to file a mandatory report?
options: Medication error that caused no harm
questions.explanation: Suspected child abuse, elder abuse, and certain communicable diseases require mandatory reporting to authorities by law to protect vulnerable populations.

title: Safety & Infection Control
description: Patient Safety, Disaster Management, Hazardous Materials, Infection Control

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client who requires contact precautions. Which of the following actions should the nurse take?
options: Wear a mask when entering the room.
Wear a gown and gloves when entering the room.
Keep the door to the client's room closed at all times.
Ensure the client wears a mask when family visits.
questions.explanation: Contact precautions require gown and gloves to prevent transmission via direct contact with the patient or contaminated surfaces.

questions.text: Which isolation precaution requires a negative pressure room?
questions.explanation: Airborne precautions require a negative pressure room to prevent airborne pathogens (TB, measles, varicella) from escaping.

questions.text: A client is prescribed a restraint. How often should the nurse release the restraint for assessment?
questions.explanation: Restraints must be released at least every 2 hours to assess circulation, skin integrity, and provide range of motion.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most effective method for preventing the spread of infection?
options: Wearing sterile gloves for all procedures
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene is the single most effective method to prevent pathogen transmission in healthcare settings.

questions.text: What is the correct order for doffing (removing) PPE?
questions.explanation: Remove most contaminated items first: Gloves, then Goggles/Face Shield, then Gown, and finally Mask (least contaminated).

questions.text: R.A.C.E. fire response protocol stands for:
questions.explanation: R.A.C.E.: Rescue clients in immediate danger, Alarm (activate fire alarm), Contain the fire (close doors), Extinguish if safe.

questions.text: During a disaster, a client arrives with a green tag. What does this indicate?
options: Immediate life-threatening injuries requiring urgent care
Delayed treatment - stable injuries that can wait
questions.explanation: Green tag = Minor injuries, walking wounded. These patients can wait and are treated last in disaster triage.

questions.text: A client with tuberculosis requires which type of mask?
options: No mask needed if room door is closed
questions.explanation: TB requires airborne precautions with an N95 respirator (or PAPR) to filter airborne particles smaller than 5 microns.

questions.text: Which client requires droplet precautions?
questions.explanation: Influenza requires droplet precautions (surgical mask, within 3 feet). TB and chickenpox need airborne; C. diff needs contact precautions.

questions.text: A nurse discovers a mercury spill from a broken thermometer. What is the priority action?
options: Evacuate the area and restrict access
Wipe up mercury with paper towels
Pour water over the spill to dilute it
questions.explanation: Mercury is hazardous. Priority is to evacuate, restrict access, and call the hazardous materials team. Never vacuum mercury.

questions.text: Which of The Joint Commission's National Patient Safety Goals addresses patient identification?
options: Use at least two patient identifiers before administering medications
questions.explanation: Using two patient identifiers (name and DOB, not room number) before procedures/medications is a core safety goal.

questions.text: A client is at high risk for falls. Which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Place the bed in the lowest position
Keep the side rails fully raised at all times
questions.explanation: Bed in lowest position reduces fall injury risk. Restraints increase fall risk; full side rails are considered restraints.

questions.text: During disaster triage, which color tag indicates the highest priority for treatment?
questions.explanation: Red tag = Immediate, life-threatening injuries requiring urgent intervention (e.g., airway obstruction, severe bleeding).

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with C. difficile. Which precaution is essential?
options: Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after contact
Wash hands with soap and water
Place client in negative pressure room
questions.explanation: C. diff spores are not killed by alcohol. Soap and water hand washing is required, along with contact precautions.

questions.text: P.A.S.S. fire extinguisher technique stands for:
questions.explanation: P.A.S.S.: Pull the pin, Aim at base of fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side.

questions.text: Which client requires airborne precautions?
questions.explanation: Measles requires airborne precautions (N95, negative pressure room). MRSA needs contact; pertussis needs droplet precautions.

questions.text: A nurse witnesses a chemical spill in the hallway. What is the first action?
options: Alert others and evacuate the area
questions.explanation: Safety first: Alert others, evacuate the area, and activate the hazardous materials response team.

questions.text: Standard precautions should be used:
options: Only for clients with known infections
For all clients at all times
questions.explanation: Standard precautions are used for ALL clients, ALL the time, regardless of diagnosis, to prevent transmission of pathogens.

questions.text: A client is receiving chemotherapy. How should the nurse handle the client's linens?
options: Double-bag in specially marked hazardous waste bags
Wash separately in hot water only
questions.explanation: Linens contaminated with chemotherapy require double-bagging in specially marked hazardous waste bags per facility protocol.

questions.text: Which factor increases a client's risk for falls?
questions.explanation: Diuretics increase fall risk due to frequent urination (rushing to bathroom), orthostatic hypotension, and electrolyte imbalances.

questions.text: During a mass casualty event, which client should be triaged as yellow (delayed)?
options: Open fracture of the femur, stable vital signs
questions.explanation: Yellow = Delayed. Serious injuries but stable vital signs; can wait for treatment (e.g., fractures without hemorrhage).

questions.text: A nurse is preparing to administer a medication and notices the client's ID band is missing. What should the nurse do?
options: Ask the client to state their name and proceed
Check the room number and proceed
Obtain a new ID band before administering medication
Ask the roommate to verify the client's identity
questions.explanation: Two identifiers are required. A new ID band must be obtained before medication administration to ensure correct patient identification.

questions.text: Which PPE is required for standard precautions when there is risk of blood splash?
options: Gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection
questions.explanation: Risk of splash to face/eyes requires full PPE: gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection (goggles or face shield).

questions.text: A client with varicella (chickenpox) requires which precautions?
questions.explanation: Varicella requires both airborne (N95, negative pressure) and contact precautions due to airborne transmission and skin lesions.

questions.text: When should a nurse perform hand hygiene? Select the BEST answer.
options: Only before and after direct patient contact
Before and after glove use, and between patients
Only when hands are visibly soiled
At the beginning and end of each shift
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene is required: before/after patient contact, before/after glove use, after touching contaminated surfaces, between patients.

title: Safety & Infection Control - Part B
description: Advanced Safety: Triage, Hazmat, Isolation Precautions, Ergonomics

questions.text: What are the two patient identifiers that must ALWAYS be used before any intervention?
options: Date of birth and medical record number
questions.explanation: Name and date of birth are the two distinct identifiers required before giving meds, drawing blood, or any intervention. Room numbers can change.

questions.text: Why is patient identification considered the 'bedrock' of patient safety?
options: It's the easiest task to perform
If you start with the wrong patient, everything after that is wrong
questions.explanation: Starting with the wrong patient makes every subsequent action wrong - wrong meds, wrong procedures, potentially catastrophic outcomes.

questions.text: What is the most foundational fall prevention intervention?
options: Ensuring the patient knows how to use their call light and it's within reach
questions.explanation: The call light must be within reach and the patient must know how to use it. If they can't reach it, provide an alternative like a handheld bell.

questions.text: Where should a confused patient who might wander be assigned?
options: At the end of the hallway for privacy
Near the nursing station to cut down response time
In a private room far from noise
questions.explanation: Confused patients who might wander need to be near the nursing station for quick response and constant monitoring.

questions.text: Approximately how many deaths per year in the US are attributed to medication errors?
questions.explanation: Up to 7,000 deaths per year in the US are attributed to medication errors, making rigid protocols essential.

questions.text: What is the 'closed loop' communication technique for medication orders?
options: Reading back verbal or handwritten orders to the physician for confirmation
questions.explanation: Closed loop communication means reading back orders to the prescriber for verification, closing off chances for misinterpretation.

questions.text: Why must latex allergy assessment include questions about specific fruits?
options: Latex proteins are similar to proteins in bananas, avocados, and papayas (cross-reactivity)
questions.explanation: Latex proteins cross-react with proteins in bananas, avocados, and papayas. Latex allergy patients often react to these fruits.

questions.text: What are the two critical 'do nots' during a seizure?
options: Do not call for help, do not document
Do not restrain the patient, do not put anything in their mouth
Do not stay with patient, do not time the seizure
Do not lower the bed, do not pad rails
questions.explanation: Never restrain (can cause fractures) and never put anything in the mouth (can block airway or cause injury). Focus on protection, not restriction.

questions.text: What does RACE stand for in fire response?
questions.explanation: RACE: Rescue patients from immediate danger, pull the Alarm, Confine the fire by closing doors, Extinguish or Evacuate.

questions.text: What does PASS stand for when using a fire extinguisher?
questions.explanation: PASS: Pull the pin, Aim at the base of the fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side.

questions.text: What is brachytherapy?
options: Radioactive source placed inside the patient's body near the tumor
questions.explanation: Brachytherapy places a radioactive source inside the patient's body, making the patient themselves radioactive and requiring strict safety protocols.

questions.text: What are the three principles for reducing radiation exposure in brachytherapy?
questions.explanation: Time (limit to 30 min/day), Distance (stay 6 feet away when possible), and Shielding (use protective barriers) reduce radiation exposure.

questions.text: Why are pregnant staff or those trying to conceive restricted from brachytherapy patients?
options: The radiation risk to the developing fetus is too high
questions.explanation: Radiation exposure poses severe risks to developing fetuses, making this restriction critical for fetal safety.

questions.text: What is the number one rule for manual lifting to prevent back injury?
options: Never bend and twist at the same time
questions.explanation: Never bend and twist simultaneously - this is the most common cause of back injuries. Use your legs, keep your back straight, and get help.

questions.text: What are the two phases of disaster recovery?
options: Restoration (getting basic services back) and Reconstruction (long-term rebuilding)
questions.explanation: Restoration focuses on temporary solutions and basic services. Reconstruction is the long-term physical and economic rebuilding.

questions.text: How long does a mass casualty triage assessment take?
questions.explanation: Triage must be rapid - 30 to 60 seconds per patient - to quickly categorize and prioritize large numbers of casualties.

questions.text: What does RED tag mean in mass casualty triage?
options: Immediate - life-threatening injuries with good survival chance if treated now
Dead or dying, no chance of survival
questions.explanation: RED (Immediate): Life-threatening injuries like shock or major burns, but good survival chance with immediate treatment.

questions.text: What does YELLOW tag mean in mass casualty triage?
options: Delayed - seriously injured but stable, can wait a few hours
questions.explanation: YELLOW (Delayed): Serious injuries like multiple fractures, but stable enough to wait a few hours without life-threatening consequences.

questions.text: What does BLACK tag mean in mass casualty triage?
options: Expectant - catastrophic injuries, not expected to survive given limited resources
questions.explanation: BLACK (Expectant): Catastrophic injuries like massive head trauma where survival is not expected given limited resources. A terrible but necessary triage decision.

questions.text: What does BE NICE stand for in hazardous materials?
options: Biological, Environmental, Nuclear, Incendiary, Chemical, Explosive
questions.explanation: BE NICE: Biological, Nuclear, Incendiary, Chemical, Explosive - the major categories of hazardous materials in disasters.

questions.text: What is the key difference in response between chemical and biological threats?
options: Chemical effects are immediate (requiring immediate decon), biological effects are delayed (requiring isolation and prophylaxis)
questions.explanation: Chemical agents (nerve gas) have immediate effects requiring immediate decontamination. Biological agents (anthrax) have delayed symptoms requiring isolation and preventive treatment.

questions.text: What are Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and why are they critical?
options: Documents that detail PPE needed, handling procedures, and emergency response for hazardous materials
questions.explanation: SDS provide essential information about hazardous materials: what PPE to wear, how to handle safely, and emergency procedures.

questions.text: What is the most common hospital-acquired infection (HAI)?
questions.explanation: Catheter-associated UTIs (CAUTI) are by far the most common nosocomial infection, followed by surgical site infections and pneumonia.

questions.text: What is the cornerstone of standard precautions?
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene is the cornerstone of standard precautions - the universal baseline for every single patient.

questions.text: When should PPE be used under standard precautions?
options: Only when you anticipate splash or spray of body fluids
questions.explanation: Under standard precautions, PPE (gloves, gowns, face shields) is based on the task and anticipated exposure, not the patient's diagnosis.

questions.text: What type of mask is required for airborne precautions?
questions.explanation: Airborne precautions (TB, chickenpox) require a fitted N95 respirator to filter tiny particles that hang in the air for long periods.

questions.text: What type of room is required for airborne precautions?
questions.explanation: Airborne precautions require a negative pressure room that pulls air in and filters it before releasing, preventing airborne particles from escaping.

questions.text: What is the key difference between airborne and droplet precautions?
options: Particle size - airborne has tiny particles that hang in air, droplet has larger particles that only travel a few feet
questions.explanation: Airborne: tiny particles that hang in air (need N95, negative pressure room). Droplet: larger particles that travel only a few feet (need surgical mask only).

questions.text: What PPE is required for contact precautions?
options: Gown and gloves every time you enter the room
questions.explanation: Contact precautions (MRSA, C. diff) require gown and gloves every single time you enter the room to prevent transmission via touch.

questions.text: What is the immediate first action after a needle stick injury?
options: Wash the site with soap and water, let it bleed
questions.explanation: Immediately wash with soap and water and let it bleed to flush out potential pathogens. Then report immediately for assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis.

questions.text: What diseases is the source patient assessed for after a needle stick?
options: HIV, Hepatitis B, and Hepatitis C
questions.explanation: The source patient is assessed for HIV, Hepatitis B, and Hepatitis C - the primary bloodborne pathogens of concern.

questions.text: What is post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) and who pays for it?
options: Preventive treatment after exposure, provided free of charge by the facility
questions.explanation: PEP is preventive treatment given after exposure to bloodborne pathogens. The facility must provide it free of charge to exposed staff.

questions.text: Why is 'layered defense' important in safety?
options: Big picture preparation (disaster plans) must merge with moment-to-moment vigilance (hand washing, ID checks)
questions.explanation: Safety requires both macro-level preparation (disaster plans, drills) and micro-level vigilance (hand hygiene, patient ID) working together.

questions.text: What makes a patient an active partner in their own safety?
options: Knowing how to use call light, recognizing infection signs, understanding medication side effects
questions.explanation: Empowering patients with knowledge about call lights, infection signs, and medication effects makes them active safety partners.

questions.text: What is the three-fold purpose of an infection control plan?
options: Decrease infection rates, evaluate effectiveness of measures, spot and manage outbreaks quickly
questions.explanation: Infection control plans must: 1) Decrease infection rates, 2) Evaluate how well measures work (e.g., hand hygiene compliance), 3) Quickly identify and manage outbreaks.

title: Health Promotion & Maintenance Part 1
description: Pediatrics, Growth & Development, Immunizations, Newborn Care

questions.text: At what age should an infant double their birth weight?
questions.explanation: Infants typically double their birth weight by 6 months and triple it by 1 year.

questions.text: According to Erikson's theory, what is the primary developmental task for infants (0-1 year)?
questions.explanation: Erikson's first stage (0-1 year) is Trust vs. Mistrust, where infants learn to trust caregivers to meet their needs.

questions.text: A newborn has an APGAR score of 8 at 1 minute. What does this indicate?
options: Good condition with minimal intervention needed
questions.explanation: APGAR 7-10 = Good condition. Score of 8 is normal; minor interventions like stimulation or oxygen may be needed.

questions.text: At what age should a child receive the MMR (measles, mumps, rubella) vaccine?
questions.explanation: MMR is given at 12-15 months (first dose) and 4-6 years (second dose). It's a live vaccine, not given before 12 months.

questions.text: Which reflex should be present in a healthy newborn?
questions.explanation: All are normal newborn reflexes. Babinski (toes fan), Moro (startle), and gag reflex should all be present.

questions.text: According to Piaget, a 2-year-old child is in which cognitive stage?
questions.explanation: Preoperational stage (2-7 years) is characterized by symbolic thinking, egocentrism, and magical thinking.

questions.text: What is the most important injury prevention measure for infants?
options: Never leave infant unattended on elevated surfaces
Use a car seat facing forward
Allow infant to sleep on stomach
questions.explanation: Falls are a major injury risk. Never leave infants unattended on changing tables, beds, or couches.

questions.text: At what age can a child typically ride a tricycle?
questions.explanation: By age 3, most children have the gross motor skills and coordination to ride a tricycle.

questions.text: Which vaccine is given at birth?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis B vaccine is given at birth (first dose), then at 1-2 months and 6-18 months.

questions.text: A 4-month-old infant should be able to:
questions.explanation: By 4 months, infants can typically roll from back to front. Sitting unsupported occurs around 6 months.

questions.text: What is the recommended sleep position for infants to prevent SIDS?
questions.explanation: 'Back to sleep' reduces SIDS risk. Infants should sleep on their backs on a firm surface without loose bedding.

questions.text: According to Erikson, what is the developmental task for toddlers (1-3 years)?
questions.explanation: Toddlers (1-3 years) develop autonomy through independence and self-control. Encouraging choices supports this stage.

questions.text: At what age should solid foods be introduced to an infant?
questions.explanation: Solid foods are introduced around 4-6 months when the infant shows readiness (sits with support, shows interest in food).

questions.text: Which finding in a newborn requires immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Central cyanosis indicates inadequate oxygenation and requires immediate intervention. Acrocyanosis is normal in newborns.

questions.text: The DTaP vaccine protects against which diseases?
questions.explanation: DTaP = Diphtheria, Tetanus, and acellular Pertussis (whooping cough). Given at 2, 4, 6, 15-18 months, and 4-6 years.

questions.text: A 6-month-old infant should be able to:
questions.explanation: By 6 months, infants can typically sit without support. Walking occurs around 12 months; first words around 12 months.

questions.text: What is the appropriate car seat position for an infant under 2 years?
options: No car seat needed if held by adult
questions.explanation: Infants and toddlers under 2 years should be rear-facing in the back seat for optimal safety.

questions.text: Which developmental milestone is expected at 12 months?
questions.explanation: By 12 months, children typically say 2-3 words. Running occurs around 18-24 months; drawing circles around 3 years.

questions.text: What is the normal respiratory rate for a newborn?
questions.explanation: Normal newborn respiratory rate is 30-60 breaths/min. Rates above 60 may indicate respiratory distress.

questions.text: According to Freud's theory, a 2-year-old is in which psychosexual stage?
questions.explanation: Anal stage (1-3 years) focuses on toilet training and control. Oral stage is 0-1 year; phallic is 3-6 years.

questions.text: Which food should NOT be given to infants under 1 year?
questions.explanation: Honey can contain botulism spores and should not be given to infants under 1 year due to immature immune systems.

questions.text: What is the expected head circumference growth in the first year of life?
questions.explanation: Head circumference increases approximately 12 cm (5 inches) in the first year, then slows significantly.

questions.text: At what age should a child be able to speak in 2-3 word sentences?
questions.explanation: By 24 months (2 years), children typically use 2-3 word sentences. Vocabulary is around 50-300 words.

questions.text: Which vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine and should NOT be given to immunocompromised children?
questions.explanation: MMR is a live vaccine and contraindicated in immunocompromised children. DTaP, Hep B, and Hib are inactivated.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of well-child visits?
options: Health promotion, disease prevention, and developmental screening
questions.explanation: Well-child visits focus on health promotion, disease prevention, growth monitoring, developmental screening, and immunizations.

questions.text: A 3-year-old child should be able to:
questions.explanation: By age 3, children can copy a circle. Tying shoes occurs around 5-6 years; writing name around 4-5 years.

questions.text: What is the most common cause of death in children ages 1-4 years?
questions.explanation: Unintentional injuries (drowning, motor vehicle accidents, falls, poisoning) are the leading cause of death in this age group.

questions.text: Which reflex disappears by 4 months of age?
questions.explanation: Rooting reflex (turns head toward touch on cheek) disappears around 4 months. Gag, blink, and cough are protective reflexes that persist.

questions.text: According to Erikson, what is the developmental task for preschoolers (3-6 years)?
questions.explanation: Preschoolers (3-6 years) develop initiative through play, exploration, and asking questions. Encourage creativity and curiosity.

questions.text: What is the recommended method for taking a temperature in a newborn?
questions.explanation: Rectal temperature is most accurate for newborns and infants under 3 months. Axillary is acceptable for screening.

title: Psychosocial Integrity - Part A
description: Therapeutic Communication, Abuse & Neglect Assessment, Family Dynamics

questions.text: What are the two non-negotiable principles of therapeutic communication?
options: Unwavering respect for the patient/family and the assumption that all behavior has meaning
questions.explanation: The foundation of therapeutic communication is unwavering respect and recognizing that all behavior - even silence or anger - is meaningful communication.

questions.text: What is the most effective way to open a therapeutic conversation?
options: Use an open-ended statement like 'Is there anything you'd like to discuss today?'
Tell them what you think they need
questions.explanation: Open-ended statements invite the patient to lead the conversation and share what's important to them, building rapport immediately.

questions.text: What is empathy through reflection?
options: Repeating exactly what the patient said
Making a concrete observation ('You're shaking') then reflecting a potential feeling ('You seem worried')
Telling them how you would feel
Asking why they feel that way
questions.explanation: Reflection combines observation with empathetic interpretation, validating nonverbal cues and giving patients a chance to confirm or clarify their feelings.

questions.text: A patient says 'This pill is just making me feel worse.' What is verbalizing the implied message?
options: Asking if they want to stop the medication
Saying 'It sounds like you feel the treatment is trapping you'
Telling them to give it more time
questions.explanation: Verbalizing the implied message gives voice to the deeper frustration or fear beneath their words, helping them feel truly heard.

questions.text: Why should you avoid saying 'Everything will be fine' to an anxious patient?
options: It's a meaningless cliché that dismisses and invalidates their fear
It makes promises you can't keep
It takes too long to say
questions.explanation: Meaningless clichés like 'Everything will be fine' are dismissive and invalidate the patient's legitimate concerns, destroying trust.

questions.text: Why is giving approval or disapproval ('I think that's great' or 'You shouldn't cry') harmful?
options: It immediately puts you in a position of judgment, destroying trust
questions.explanation: Approval/disapproval creates a judgmental dynamic that destroys the therapeutic relationship and makes patients hide their true feelings.

questions.text: Why should you avoid asking 'Why are you in tears?'
options: It puts the patient on the defensive and makes them justify their feelings
questions.explanation: 'Why' questions force patients to defend or justify their emotions, creating defensiveness instead of openness.

questions.text: Instead of asking 'why,' what should you ask to understand a patient's distress?
options: Focus on 'what' and feelings: 'Tell me what led up to this' or 'Help me understand what you're feeling'
questions.explanation: 'What' questions and feeling-focused inquiries let patients be the expert on their own experience without defensiveness.

questions.text: A patient says 'The night nurse promised I didn't have to walk today.' What should you do FIRST?
options: Show them the physical therapy order
Acknowledge their feeling: 'I understand you feel let down'
questions.explanation: Always acknowledge the emotion BEFORE dealing with facts. Address feelings first, then problem-solve.

questions.text: How should communication be adapted for patients with cognitive disabilities?
options: Use concrete, simple ideas in a quiet area, possibly with visual aids
questions.explanation: Cognitive disabilities require concrete, simple communication in a calm environment, often supplemented with visual aids.

questions.text: What is the priority when communicating with a patient in an acute psychiatric state?
options: Safety first - speak calmly, use direct eye contact, never challenge them if paranoid
questions.explanation: Safety is paramount. Use calm, direct communication and never challenge paranoid delusions as this escalates danger.

questions.text: What is the biggest red flag for abuse in a patient's history?
options: Inconsistency between physical findings and the story being told
questions.explanation: A glaring inconsistency between injuries and the explanation (e.g., spiral fracture from 'rolling off sofa') is a major abuse indicator.

questions.text: What behavioral patterns suggest possible abuse?
options: Patient denies how they got hurt, or family is either indifferent or over-the-top
questions.explanation: Denial about injuries, family indifference, or excessive concern can all indicate abuse dynamics.

questions.text: What financial indicator suggests elder abuse?
options: Patient lacks access to their own money, or a family member controls everything
questions.explanation: Financial exploitation is common in elder abuse - look for lack of access to their own funds or family members controlling finances.

questions.text: What physical findings suggest abuse?
options: Pattern bruising (belt/rope marks), cigarette burns, bilateral injuries (defensive wounds)
questions.explanation: Pattern injuries, burns, and bilateral defensive wounds are classic physical indicators of intentional harm.

questions.text: What is a torn frenulum and why is it significant?
options: Torn tissue under the lip/tongue - major indicator of force-feeding or direct blow in children
questions.explanation: A torn frenulum (tissue under lip/tongue) is a specific, serious indicator of force-feeding or facial trauma in children.

questions.text: What are signs of neglect?
options: Improper clothes for weather, poor hygiene, missing assistive devices, unexplained weight loss
questions.explanation: Neglect shows as missing basic needs: appropriate clothing, hygiene, necessary medical devices, and adequate nutrition.

questions.text: What is the FIRST step in the seven-step domestic violence protocol?
options: Inquiry and screening - ask directly 'Have you ever been abused?' and believe them
questions.explanation: Building trust starts with direct inquiry and believing the patient's disclosure, creating a safe space for them to open up.

questions.text: When does mandatory reporting override the seven-step process?
options: When there's immediate threat to life or child abuse - safety trumps everything
questions.explanation: Immediate life threats and child abuse require immediate mandatory reporting - safety always comes first.

questions.text: After mandatory reporting (if needed), what are the next steps in domestic violence intervention?
options: Validate their experience, give information (hotlines/shelters), make referrals, then document
questions.explanation: The sequence is: validate, inform, refer, document. Each step builds support and creates a paper trail.

questions.text: Why must abuse documentation be meticulous?
options: It's crucial for legal proceedings - use body maps to chart every injury
questions.explanation: Precise documentation with body maps creates legal evidence that can protect victims and prosecute abusers.

questions.text: What does family systems theory teach us?
options: The family is like a mobile - touch one part and the whole thing moves
questions.explanation: Family systems theory shows that one person's illness or crisis affects the entire family system, which seeks equilibrium.

questions.text: What does structural-functional family theory assess?
options: How well the family performs essential jobs: affection, socialization, healthcare
questions.explanation: This theory evaluates whether the family is fulfilling its core functions - when these break down, dysfunction appears.

questions.text: What are red flags for dysfunctional family coping?
options: Substance abuse, scapegoating one member, denial that problems exist
questions.explanation: Substance abuse, scapegoating, and denial are classic signs of dysfunctional family coping mechanisms.

questions.text: What is the biggest mistake clinicians make about Kubler-Ross's five stages of grief?
options: Thinking of them as linear stages like a ladder, when grief is actually messy and non-linear
questions.explanation: Grief is not linear - patients can move between denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance in any order, repeatedly.

questions.text: What is anticipatory grief?
options: Grief felt before a loss happens, like grieving for a terminally ill loved one while they're still alive
questions.explanation: Anticipatory grief occurs before the actual loss, as people grieve the impending death of a loved one.

questions.text: What is disenfranchised grief?
options: Grief that society doesn't acknowledge or support (e.g., losing a pet, end of an affair)
questions.explanation: Disenfranchised grief lacks social recognition and support, forcing people to grieve alone (pet loss, affair ending, etc.).

questions.text: What's the difference between moderate and severe anxiety?
options: Moderate narrows focus slightly; severe causes tunnel vision and inability to perform simple tasks
questions.explanation: Moderate anxiety narrows focus somewhat, but severe anxiety creates tunnel vision on one detail and prevents basic functioning.

questions.text: What characterizes panic-level anxiety?
options: Complete loss of function, terror, dread, overwhelming physical symptoms
questions.explanation: Panic is a complete breakdown of function with overwhelming terror, dread, and severe physical manifestations.

questions.text: What is the number one predictor of future suicide attempts?
questions.explanation: Previous suicide attempts are the strongest predictor of future attempts - this history must always be taken seriously.

questions.text: What other factors increase suicide risk?
options: Lack of social support, hopelessness, substance use, chronic painful medical conditions
questions.explanation: Isolation, hopelessness, substance abuse, and chronic suffering all significantly elevate suicide risk.

questions.text: What is the first rule of de-escalation with an agitated patient?
questions.explanation: Safety requires maintaining 3-12 feet of distance while using a calm voice to de-escalate.

questions.text: In de-escalation, what should you validate?
options: Their feeling, but NOT their behavior ('I can see you're very upset')
questions.explanation: Validate emotions ('You're upset') but never validate dangerous or inappropriate behavior.

questions.text: Why should you ignore challenging questions during de-escalation?
options: Arguing about 'why you're here' or 'who's in charge' adds fuel to the fire
questions.explanation: Engaging with challenging questions escalates conflict - stay focused on calming and safety.

questions.text: When are restraints appropriate?
options: Always as a last resort after everything else fails, with physician's order
questions.explanation: Restraints are ALWAYS a last resort, only after all other interventions fail, and require a physician's order.

title: Psychosocial Integrity - Part B
description: Restraints, CBT, Trauma-Informed Care, Crisis Management

questions.text: How often must patients in physical restraints be checked?
questions.explanation: Physical restraints require checks every 1-2 hours to assess circulation, skin integrity, hydration, nutrition, and prevent complications like blood clots.

questions.text: What must be assessed during restraint monitoring?
options: Circulation, skin integrity, ability to get food/water, preventing blood clots and tissue damage
questions.explanation: Comprehensive monitoring includes circulation, skin condition, hydration, nutrition, and prevention of serious complications.

questions.text: Do chemical restraints (medications) require the same monitoring as physical restraints?
options: Yes - nurses must assess and document patient status at least every 2 hours
questions.explanation: Chemical restraints require the same rigorous monitoring - assessment and documentation at least every 2 hours.

questions.text: What is the core principle guiding restraint use?
options: Always use the least restrictive measure possible to keep everyone safe - never for convenience
questions.explanation: The guiding principle is least restrictive intervention for safety - restraints are NEVER for staff convenience.

questions.text: What is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) based on?
options: The link between thoughts, feelings, and behaviors
questions.explanation: CBT focuses on the interconnection between thoughts, feelings, and behaviors, helping patients identify maladaptive patterns.

questions.text: What is Aaron Beck's 'cognitive triad'?
options: Negative view of self, the world, and the future
questions.explanation: Beck's cognitive triad describes the three negative thought patterns in depression: negative views of self, world, and future.

questions.text: A patient says 'I'm a failure, I mess everything up' after one setback. What CBT technique helps?
options: Reframing: 'That particular skill didn't work this time. What can we learn?' - shifting from permanent flaw to temporary problem
questions.explanation: CBT reframing helps patients see setbacks as temporary, solvable problems rather than permanent character flaws.

questions.text: What is Trauma-Informed Care (TIC)?
options: A fundamental framework recognizing how common trauma is, built on five pillars
questions.explanation: TIC is a comprehensive care framework that recognizes trauma's prevalence and restructures care around five core pillars.

questions.text: What are the five pillars of Trauma-Informed Care?
questions.explanation: TIC's five pillars are: Safety (physical/emotional), Trustworthiness, Choice (autonomy), Collaboration, and Empowerment.

questions.text: Why is 'choice' important in Trauma-Informed Care, even over small things?
options: Giving autonomy over small decisions restores a sense of control to trauma survivors
questions.explanation: Trauma often involves loss of control - offering choices, even small ones, helps restore autonomy and empowerment.

questions.text: What does 'collaboration' mean in TIC?
options: Working WITH patients, not doing things TO them
questions.explanation: Collaboration means partnering with patients as equals, not imposing care on them - a fundamental power shift.

questions.text: What is the focus of 'empowerment' in TIC?
options: Focusing on their strengths and helping them advocate for themselves
questions.explanation: Empowerment builds on patient strengths and develops their self-advocacy skills, promoting recovery and resilience.

questions.text: How does TIC change the power dynamic in healthcare?
options: It transforms the relationship into a true partnership
It gives all power to patients
questions.explanation: TIC fundamentally shifts from a hierarchical model to a collaborative partnership between provider and patient.

questions.text: What is the ultimate goal of psychosocial integrity mastery?
options: Balancing technical knowledge (restraint rules) with deep human interaction skills
questions.explanation: True mastery combines technical competence with genuine human connection - both are essential for effective care.

questions.text: Why must you treat patients as 'whole persons' in psychosocial care?
options: Because they exist in families, deal with loss, and require you to be both communicator and investigator
questions.explanation: Patients are complex beings in social contexts - effective care requires seeing the whole person, not just symptoms.

questions.text: What common phrase should you rephrase to show real empathy?
options: 'Don't worry about it' - this dismisses concerns rather than validating them
questions.explanation: Phrases like 'Don't worry' dismiss valid concerns - instead, acknowledge feelings and offer support.

questions.text: What makes silence meaningful in therapeutic communication?
options: Silence is communication - it can indicate fear, processing, resistance, or comfort
It means the patient is fine
questions.explanation: Silence communicates volumes - it may show fear, deep thought, resistance, or peaceful acceptance. Never dismiss it.

questions.text: Why is anger considered meaningful communication?
options: Anger often masks fear, loss of control, or unmet needs - it's a signal to investigate
questions.explanation: Anger is rarely just anger - it usually signals underlying fear, powerlessness, or unaddressed needs requiring exploration.

questions.text: What does non-compliance often communicate?
options: Lack of understanding, fear, cultural barriers, or loss of autonomy
questions.explanation: Non-compliance is meaningful behavior signaling barriers like confusion, fear, cultural issues, or feeling powerless.

questions.text: How do you build rapport with a patient who won't talk?
options: Respect the silence, make observations, reflect potential feelings, give them control
questions.explanation: Respect silence, use gentle observations and reflections, and empower them to open up when ready - never force communication.

questions.text: What is the relationship between psychosocial integrity and patient safety?
options: Poor psychosocial care increases safety risks - agitation, non-compliance, self-harm
questions.explanation: Psychosocial distress directly impacts safety through agitation, treatment refusal, and self-harm risk.

questions.text: Why is therapeutic communication considered a 'clinical skill' not just 'being nice'?
options: It requires specific techniques, assessment, and directly impacts outcomes and safety
questions.explanation: Therapeutic communication uses evidence-based techniques that require training and directly affect patient outcomes and safety.

questions.text: What connects abuse assessment to psychosocial integrity?
options: Abuse creates profound psychosocial trauma requiring specialized communication and trust-building
questions.explanation: Abuse causes deep psychosocial wounds - effective assessment requires therapeutic communication skills and trauma-informed approaches.

questions.text: How does understanding grief stages help in crisis intervention?
options: Recognizing grief responses helps normalize patient reactions and guide appropriate support
questions.explanation: Understanding grief helps validate patient experiences, normalize their reactions, and provide appropriate, stage-sensitive support.

questions.text: Why is family systems theory important in psychosocial care?
options: One person's crisis affects the whole family system - you must assess and support the entire unit
questions.explanation: Family systems theory shows that individual crises ripple through the entire family - effective care addresses the whole system.

questions.text: What is the connection between anxiety levels and intervention approaches?
options: All anxiety is treated the same
Mild/moderate anxiety needs different interventions than severe/panic - escalation requires immediate crisis protocols
questions.explanation: Intervention intensity must match anxiety level - mild needs support, severe/panic requires immediate crisis intervention.

questions.text: Why must suicide risk assessment be thorough and ongoing?
options: Risk fluctuates - previous attempts, current stressors, and protective factors change constantly
questions.explanation: Suicide risk is dynamic - factors like support, hopelessness, and stressors change, requiring continuous reassessment.

questions.text: What makes de-escalation a critical psychosocial skill?
options: It prevents violence, maintains therapeutic relationships, and avoids traumatic restraint use
questions.explanation: Effective de-escalation prevents violence, preserves the therapeutic relationship, and avoids the trauma of restraints.

questions.text: How does CBT empower patients in their own care?
options: It teaches them to identify and change their own thought patterns, giving them tools for self-management
questions.explanation: CBT gives patients concrete skills to recognize and modify their own thoughts and behaviors, promoting autonomy and recovery.

questions.text: Why is Trauma-Informed Care considered a 'framework' rather than a 'technique'?
options: It fundamentally restructures ALL care delivery around trauma awareness, not just specific interventions
questions.explanation: TIC is a comprehensive framework that transforms the entire care approach, environment, and relationships - not just isolated techniques.

title: Basic Care & Comfort - Part A
description: Mobility, Rehab Settings, Assistive Devices, Gait Patterns

questions.text: Which rehabilitation setting is appropriate for a patient on mechanical ventilation needing complex wound care?
questions.explanation: LTACHs are for medically fragile patients who still need intensive support like vents or complex wound care, but are transitioning from the ICU/Acute care.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between Physical Therapy (PT) and Occupational Therapy (OT)?
options: PT is for legs, OT is for arms
PT focuses on gross motor/mobility (walking), OT focuses on ADLs (bathing, dressing, living skills)
PT is for hospital, OT is for home
questions.explanation: PT targets big movements (mobility, ambulation), while OT targets 'occupations' of daily life (ADLs/IADLs like dressing, cooking).

questions.text: When fitting a cane or crutches, where should the top of the device line up?
options: The patient's wrist crease when standing straight
questions.explanation: The wrist crease is the correct landmark. This ensures a 15-degree elbow flexion for optimal leverage and stability.

questions.text: What does the 'Four-Point Gait' pattern look like?
options: Move both crutches then both legs
Right crutch, Left foot, Left crutch, Right foot
Move one crutch and opposite foot together
questions.explanation: Four-point gait is slow but stable: Right crutch, Left foot, Left crutch, Right foot. Used for patients who can bear weight on both legs.

questions.text: Which gait pattern mimics normal walking rhythm?
options: Two-point gait (Crutch and opposite foot move together)
questions.explanation: Two-point gait speeds things up by moving the crutch and opposite leg simultaneously, mimicking a natural arm-swing walking rhythm.

questions.text: A patient cannot bear weight on their left leg. Which gait pattern is appropriate?
questions.explanation: Three-point gait is for non-weight bearing on one leg. Both crutches and the bad leg move forward, then the good leg follows.

questions.text: What is the rule for going UP stairs with crutches?
options: Up with the GOOD leg first
questions.explanation: 'Up with the Good, Down with the Bad.' The good leg goes up first to lift the body.

questions.text: What is 'Scheduled Voiding' in bladder training?
options: Going when the patient feels the urge
Toileting at fixed time intervals (e.g., every 3 hours) regardless of urge
Asking the patient if they are dry
questions.explanation: Scheduled voiding is strictly clock-based to prevent accidents by emptying the bladder before it gets too full.

questions.text: What is 'The Knack' for stress incontinence?
options: Consciously squeezing pelvic floor muscles BEFORE coughing or sneezing
questions.explanation: The Knack is a pre-emptive pelvic floor contraction (Kegel) right before increasing abdominal pressure (cough/sneeze) to prevent leakage.

questions.text: Which type of fiber 'slows things down' by forming a gel?
questions.explanation: Soluble fiber dissolves in water to form a gel, which can help manage diarrhea and regulate transit. Insoluble fiber adds bulk.

questions.text: What is the maximum angle for the head of the bed to prevent shear?
questions.explanation: Keep the HOB at 30 degrees or less when possible to prevent gravity from pulling the skeleton down while skin sticks to sheets (shear).

questions.text: How often should a patient be repositioned in a CHAIR?
questions.explanation: Chair sitting puts intense pressure on ischial tuberosities. Repositioning (weight shifting) is required every 15 minutes.

questions.text: What defines a Stage 1 Pressure Injury?
questions.explanation: Stage 1 is intact skin with redness that does not turn white (blanch) when pressed. It's the warning signal.
