questions.text: What does a 'Suspected Deep Tissue Injury' look like?
options: Deep purple or maroon area of intact skin
questions.explanation: DTI appears as a purple/maroon bruise on intact skin, indicating serious damage to underlying soft tissue from pressure/shear.

questions.text: What prevents staging a pressure ulcer?
options: The wound bed is covered by slough (yellow) or eschar (black)
questions.explanation: If you cannot see the bottom of the wound due to necrotic tissue (slough/eschar), it is Unstageable.

questions.text: What is the 30-degree lateral position?
options: Lying completely on the side (90 degrees)
Tilted 30 degrees to the side, supported by pillows, to stay off the sacrum AND hip bone
questions.explanation: This position offloads the sacrum without putting direct pressure on the greater trochanter (hip bone), unlike a full 90-degree turn.

questions.text: How does TENS therapy work to relieve pain?
options: It stimulates large nerve fibers to 'close the gate' on pain signals and releases endorphins
questions.explanation: TENS uses electrical impulses to block pain transmission (Gate Control Theory) and stimulate natural endorphins.

questions.text: Why is St. John's Wort a safety concern?
options: It interacts with many drugs (anticoagulants, birth control) making them ineffective
questions.explanation: St. John's Wort induces liver enzymes that metabolize drugs faster, reducing the effectiveness of critical meds like Warfarin and contraceptives.

questions.text: What is Algor Mortis?
options: Cooling of the body after death
questions.explanation: Algor Mortis is the cooling of the body (~1 degree C per hour) until it matches room temperature.

questions.text: What is Livor Mortis?
questions.explanation: Livor Mortis is the pooling of blood in dependent areas, causing discoloration. It becomes fixed after ~6 hours.

questions.text: When does Rigor Mortis typically start?
options: 2-4 hours after death, starting in small muscles
questions.explanation: Rigor (stiffening) begins in small muscles (jaw/neck) at 2-4 hours, peaks at 8-10 hours.

questions.text: What are the criteria for death pronouncement?
options: No pulse or respirations for 60 seconds, no pupil response, no reflexes
questions.explanation: Assessment requires checking for absence of pulse and breathing for a full 60 seconds, plus lack of responsiveness/reflexes.

questions.text: How should a body be positioned post-mortem?
options: Supine with head slightly elevated (prevent discoloration), eyes closed, dentures in
questions.explanation: Position naturally, head elevated to prevent pooling in face, close eyes, insert dentures (if policy allows/possible) for dignity.

questions.text: What is prompt voiding used for?
questions.explanation: Prompted voiding works well for cognitive impairment: regular checks + asking 'Are you dry?' + positive reinforcement.

questions.text: What causes shearing force?
options: Skeleton sliding down while skin stays stuck to surface
questions.explanation: Shear is the mechanical force of parallel opposites: bone moves down (gravity), skin stays put (friction), tearing deep vessels.

questions.text: Which heat therapy effect promotes healing?
questions.explanation: Heat causes vasodilation, bringing oxygen, nutrients, and immune cells to the area to speed healing.

questions.text: What is the purpose of visualization in pain control?
options: To distract the brain with sensory details, overriding pain signals
questions.explanation: Detailed guided imagery creates a competing sensory input that can block or dampen pain perception.

questions.text: What is a 'Subacute' care setting?
options: A bridge between hospital and home - skilled nursing but less intensive than LTACH
questions.explanation: Subacute care is for stable patients who still need daily skilled nursing/therapy but not the high-tech support of LTACH.

questions.text: What does a Stage 2 pressure injury involve?
options: Partial thickness loss (blister or shallow crater)
questions.explanation: Stage 2 involves breaks in the skin (epidermis/dermis) presenting as a blister, abrasion, or shallow crater.

questions.text: What is the nurse's role when family is viewing the body?
options: Prepare them for what to expect, provide privacy, be present for support
questions.explanation: The nurse facilitates the process: preparing the body/room, explaining (simply) what they will see, and offering quiet support.

title: Basic Care & Comfort - Part B
description: Skin Integrity, Pain Management, End of Life Care Scenarios

questions.text: What characterizes a Stage 3 pressure injury?
options: Full thickness skin loss involving damage to subcutaneous tissue (fat visible)
questions.explanation: Stage 3 extends into the subcutaneous tissue (fat is visible), but does NOT expose muscle, tendon, or bone (which would be Stage 4).

questions.text: Correctly identifying a Stage 4 pressure injury requires seeing:
questions.explanation: Stage 4 is the deepest injury, involving full-thickness loss with exposed support structures like muscle, tendon, or bone.

questions.text: What is the primary physiologic effect of Cold therapy?
options: Vasoconstriction (decreased blood flow), reduced inflammation, numbing
questions.explanation: Cold causes vasoconstriction, which limits swelling/inflammation and creates a local anesthetic (numbing) effect.

questions.text: How long should heat or cold therapy generally be applied to avoid tissue damage?
questions.explanation: Limit application to 15-20 minutes to prevent rebound effects (vasodilation/constriction reversal) or tissue injury/burns.

questions.text: For a patient with cognitive impairment preventing bladder control, which intervention is best?
questions.explanation: Prompted voiding is the evidence-based behavioral intervention for cognitive impairment, promoting continence creates dignity.

questions.text: When helping a patient with proper crutch fit stand up, the elbows should be flexed at:
questions.explanation: A 15-30 degree bend allows the arm muscles to effectively push down and lift the body weight during ambulation.

questions.text: A patient asks why they need to visualize 'smelling the ocean' during guided imagery. You explain:
options: Detailed sensory input engages the brain, distracting it from processing pain signals
questions.explanation: Multisensory detail (sight, sound, smell, feel) overloads the sensory pathways, competing with and dampening pain transmission.

questions.text: What differentiates Friction from Shear?
options: Friction is surface rubbing (abrasion); Shear is deep tissue tearing from bone/skin moving opposite directions
questions.explanation: Friction damages the surface (epidermis). Shear damages deep vessels at the bone interface, leading to deep tissue injury (DTI).

questions.text: In the 'Gate Control Theory' of pain, what does TENS do?
options: Stimulates large non-pain fibers to 'close the gate', blocking pain signals to the brain
questions.explanation: TENS stimulates large A-beta fibers which inhibit (gate) the transmission of pain signals from smaller C-fibers to the brain.

questions.text: Before starting a post-mortem bed bath, the nurse should:
options: Verify if the family has specific cultural/religious rituals for handling the body
questions.explanation: Cultural/religious needs take priority. Some cultures prohibit non-family touching the body or have specific washing rituals.

questions.text: How does 'Habit Training' differ from 'Scheduled Voiding'?
options: It is based on the patient's OWN natural pattern (determined by logging) rather than a fixed arbitrary interval
questions.explanation: Habit training is personalized based on voiding logs, whereas scheduled voiding is acceptable for those who can't report but is rigid.

questions.text: Why are 'donuts' (ring cushions) contraindicated for pressure ulcer prevention?
options: They create a ring of high pressure that restricts blood flow to the center area
questions.explanation: Donut cushions cause venous congestion and ischemia in the center circle, worsening pressure risks rather than helping.

questions.text: What indicates a patient is using the 'Three-Point Gait' correctly?
options: They advance both crutches and the weak leg together, then bring the strong leg forward
questions.explanation: The signature of 3-point gait is moving the assistive device and the injured limb simultaneously to protect it from weight-bearing.

questions.text: When is an autopsy typically mandatory?
options: Unattended death, suspicious circumstances, or death within 24 hours of hospital admission
questions.explanation: Medical Examiner cases (homicide, suicide, accident, death <24hrs of admission) require autopsy by law; tubes/lines must remain in place.

questions.text: Why do we elevate the head slightly post-mortem?
options: To prevent blood from pooling in the face (discoloration)
questions.explanation: Preventing Livor Mortis (discoloration) in visible areas like the face is a key part of maintaining dignity for viewing.

questions.text: What is the specific danger of herbal supplements like Ginkgo Biloba or Garlic?
questions.explanation: Many 'G' herbs (Garlic, Ginger, Ginkgo, Ginseng) increase bleeding risk, which is critical to know before surgery.

questions.text: Which patient requires a 'mechanical lift' (Hoyer)?
options: Patient can stand but is weak
Patient cannot bear weight and is unable to assist with transfer
questions.explanation: Zero-lift policies protect staff. If a patient cannot bear weight or assist, a full mechanical lift is required to prevent back injury.

questions.text: What is the correct way to descend stairs with a cane?
options: Cane and WEAK leg go down first
Strong leg helps lowers the body
questions.explanation: The cane goes down with the weak leg to provide support, while the strong leg bends to lower the body weight securely.

questions.text: For a client with Right-sided weakness (stroke), where should the cane be held?
questions.explanation: The cane is always held on the STRONG side (Left) to provide a wide base of support for the weak side (Right).

questions.text: What is the main goal of Palliative Care vs Hospice?
options: Palliative can occur alongside curative treatment; Hospice implies <6 months left and stopping curative treatment
questions.explanation: Palliative care focuses on symptom relief at ANY stage of illness. Hospice is a specific Medicare benefit for the end of life (<6mo) without curative intent.

title: Pharmacology Part A: Foundations & Cardiovascular
description: PK/PD, Medication Safety, Diuretics, Digoxin, Anticoagulants

questions.text: Pharmacokinetics (PK) describes:
options: What the drug does to the body
What the body does to the drug (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion)
questions.explanation: PK is the movement of drugs through the body - Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, and Excretion (ADME).

questions.text: What is the definition of a drug's Half-Life?
options: Time required for the plasma concentration of the drug to decrease by 50%
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time it takes for the amount of drug in the body to reduce by half, which determines dosing frequency.

questions.text: How many half-lives does it typically take to reach a 'Steady State' concentration?
questions.explanation: It takes consistently about 5 half-lives of regular dosing to reach a steady state where intake matches elimination.

questions.text: What is the 'First Pass Effect'?
options: Being the first to take a drug
Metabolism of a drug by the liver BEFORE it reaches systemic circulation (oral route)
questions.explanation: Orally administered drugs go from the gut -> portal vein -> liver. The liver metabolizes a chunk of it before it ever hits the bloodstream.

questions.text: What is an Agonist?
options: A drug that blocks a receptor
A drug that binds to a receptor and stimulates/activates a response
questions.explanation: Agonists are the 'keys' that turn the lock and open the door (activate the receptor).

questions.text: What is the primary danger of the Intravenous (IV) route compared to Oral?
options: Immediate bioavailability means NO time to correct errors or stop absorption if toxicity occurs
questions.explanation: IV is 100% bioavailable instantly. Once it's in, you can't get it back. Risk of toxicity and reaction is highest.

questions.text: Loop Diuretics (e.g., Furosemide) act on the Loop of Henle and are notorious for causing:
questions.explanation: Loop diuretics inhibit sodium reabsorption but also dump Potassium. Hypokalemia is a major risk.

questions.text: Potassium-Sparing Diuretics (e.g., Spironolactone) carry the risk of:
questions.explanation: They spare potassium while excreting sodium/water, so the risk is holding onto TOO much potassium (Hyperkalemia).

questions.text: Patients taking Thiazide diuretics (e.g., Hydrochlorothiazide) must be educated about:
questions.explanation: Photosensitivity is a classic side effect of thiazides. Patients need sunscreen/protective clothing.

questions.text: What is the therapeutic window for Digoxin?
questions.explanation: Digoxin has a very narrow safety margin: 0.5 - 2.0 ng/mL. Anything above 2.0 is toxic.

questions.text: A classic visual sign of Digoxin Toxicity is:
options: Seeing yellow or green halos around lights
questions.explanation: Yellow-green halos (visual disturbances) + nausea/vomiting are the hallmark signs of Dig toxicity.

questions.text: Before giving Digoxin, the nurse MUST:
options: Check Apical Pulse for full 60 seconds (hold if <60 bpm)
questions.explanation: Since Digoxin slows the heart rate, you must verify the apical pulse is >60. If it's slow, holding the dose prevents dangerous bradycardia.

questions.text: Heparin works by activating Antithrombin III and is monitored using:
questions.explanation: Heparin's effectiveness is measured by the aPTT. (Think: H-e-p-a-r-i-n + P-T-T are the two 'fingers' together).

questions.text: The antidote for Heparin overdose is:
questions.explanation: Protamine Sulfate reverses Heparin. (Vitamin K reverses Warfarin).

questions.text: Warfarin (Coumadin) acts by inhibiting Vitamin K clotting factors and is monitored using:
questions.explanation: Warfarin is monitored via INR. Therapeutic range is typically 2.0 - 3.0.

questions.text: St. John's Wort interacts with many drugs (like Birth Control & HIV meds) by:
options: Inducing liver enzymes (CYP450) which speeds up metabolism, making the drugs ineffective
questions.explanation: St. John's Wort is an enzyme INDUCER. It makes the liver 'eat' other drugs faster, clearing them before they can work.

questions.text: Ginkgo Biloba should be avoided in patients taking anticoagulants because:
questions.explanation: Ginkgo has anti-platelet effects. Combining it with Warfarin/Aspirin dramatically increases bleeding risk.

questions.text: Thrombolytics (tPA, Alteplase) are used to:
options: Dissolve ('bust') existing clots in emergencies (Stroke, MI, PE)
questions.explanation: Thrombolytics are 'clot busters' - they break down fibrin to dissolve clots that are already there.

questions.text: What is an absolute contraindication for Thrombolytic therapy?
options: Active internal bleeding or history of hemorrhagic stroke/recent head trauma
questions.explanation: Because they dissolve clots systemically, the risk of fatal hemorrhage means they are absolutely forbidden if there's bleeding/head trauma history.

questions.text: What do ACE Inhibitors (ending in -pril) primarily do?
options: Lower blood pressure by blocking Angiotensin converting enzyme
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors block the RAAS system, lowering blood pressure and reducing cardiac workload.

questions.text: Beta Blockers (ending in -lol) work by:
options: Blocking adrenaline effects (lowering HR and BP)
questions.explanation: Beta Blockers 'block' the beta receptors from adrenaline/epinephrine, slowing the heart and lowering BP.

questions.text: Calcium Channel Blockers (e.g., Verapamil, Diltiazem) work as:
questions.explanation: They block calcium entry into smooth muscle, causing relaxation (vasodilation) of arteries.

questions.text: What defines a Pharmacokinetic Drug Interaction?
options: One drug altering the absorption, metabolism, or excretion of another drug
questions.explanation: PK interactions change 'how much' drug is in the body (e.g., by changing metabolism speed).

questions.text: What is the 'Five Rights' of medication administration?
options: Right hand, Left Hand, Mouth, Eye, Ear
questions.explanation: Right Patient, Right Drug, Right Dose, Right Route, Right Time. The safety mantra.

questions.text: Why is 'Enterohepatic Recirculation' significant?
options: It reabsorbs drugs from bile back into blood, prolonging their effect
questions.explanation: It's a loop: Liver -> Bile -> Gut -> Reabsorbed -> Liver. This cycle keeps the drug in the system much longer.

questions.text: Digoxin strengthens contraction (inotrope) but also:
questions.explanation: Digoxin is a positive inotrope (stronger beat) but a negative chronotrope (slower rate). Efficient pumping, more rest.

questions.text: How does Warfarin differ from Heparin in onset?
options: Warfarin takes days (delayed onset); Heparin is immediate
questions.explanation: Warfarin stops Vitamin K production, but doesn't kill existing factors. It takes days to work. Heparin works NOW.

questions.text: What electrolyte imbalance makes Digoxin toxicity MORE likely?
questions.explanation: Low potassium (hypokalemia) sensitizes the heart to Digoxin, greatly increasing toxicity risk.

questions.text: Which drug class is used for 'Rescue' in Asthma?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is the rescue drug (SABA). It opens airways immediately.

questions.text: Why is 'First Pass Metabolism' an issue for dosing?
options: You must account for the drug lost in the liver; Oral doses are often higher than IV doses
questions.explanation: Because the liver destroys a % of the drug right away, oral doses must be high enough to ensure enough 'survives' to reach the body.

title: Pharmacology Part B: Systems & Safety
description: Endocrine (Insulin), Respiratory, Immunologic, Chemo, Herbal Safety

questions.text: Metformin primarily works by:
options: Stimulating the pancreas to squeeze more insulin
Increasing insulin sensitivity and decreasing liver glucose production
questions.explanation: Metformin is a Biguanide. It sensitizes cells to insulin and tells the liver to stop dumping sugar into the blood. No hypoglycemia risk alone.

questions.text: Rapid-Acting Insulin (Homalog/Lispro) has an onset of:
questions.explanation: Fastest insulin. Food MUST be in front of the patient. Onset 5-15 min.

questions.text: Long-Acting Insulin (Glargine/Lantus) is unique because:
options: It has NO peak (steady plateau for ~24 hours)
questions.explanation: Glargine has no peak. It provides a steady basal rate of insulin coverage for 24 hours. Never mix it in a syringe.

questions.text: Corticosteroids (Prednisone) reduce inflammation but cause:
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids powerfully suppress the immune system. Long term use leads to Cushing's, infection risk, hyperglycemia, and bone loss.

questions.text: What is 'Neutropenia' in chemotherapy patients?
options: Low Neutrophils (WBCs), leading to life-threatening infection risk
questions.explanation: Chemo kills fast-growing cells, including bone marrow. Neutropenia means the body has no defense against bacteria -> Sepsis risk.

questions.text: Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressant used to:
questions.explanation: Cyclosporine targets T-lymphocytes to stop the body from attacking a new organ.

questions.text: Chemotherapy (Alkylating agents) target:
options: ALL rapidly dividing cells (Cancer, Hair, GI tract, Bone Marrow)
questions.explanation: Chemo attacks rapid division. This explains the side effects: Hair loss (follicles), Nausea (GI lining), Infection (Bone marrow).

questions.text: Regular Insulin (Short Acting) is the only insulin that can be given:
questions.explanation: Regular insulin is the only one safe for IV administration (e.g., for DKA).

questions.text: Beta Adrenergic Agonists (Albuterol) cause bronchodilation but side effects include:
questions.explanation: Because they mimic adrenaline (sympathetic NS stimulation), they speed up the heart and cause jitters.

questions.text: Why must patients on long-term steroids NOT stop abruptly?
options: Adrenal Crisis (The body stopped making its own cortisol)
questions.explanation: Exogenous steroids supress the HPA axis. Stopping suddenly leads to Adrenal Crisis because the body can't produce cortisol fast enough.

questions.text: Theophylline (Methylxanthine) is a bronchodilator with a risk of:
questions.explanation: Like Digoxin, Theophylline has a narrow window. Toxicity prevents as nervousness, seizures, and arrhythmias.

questions.text: NPH Insulin is intermediate-acting and appears:
questions.explanation: NPH is Cloudy. (Remember: 'Clear before Cloudy' when mixing).

questions.text: What is the primary role of ACE Inhibitors in Heart Failure?
questions.explanation: By blocking Angiotensin II (a potent vasoconstrictor), ACEs open arteries, making it easier for the weak heart to pump.

questions.text: Which class of drugs is used to 'dry up' secretions (Anticholinergic)?
questions.explanation: Anticholinergics block acetylcholine, leading to 'Can't See, Can't Pee, Can't Spit, Can't Sh*t' effects.

questions.text: What is the 'synergy' of using a Loop Diuretic + Potassium-Sparing Diuretic?
options: Balancing Potassium loss (Loop) with Potassium retention (Sparing)
questions.explanation: They cancel out each other's potassium side effects while both removing fluid.

questions.text: A patient on Warfarin has an INR of 5.0 (High). The risk is:
questions.explanation: High INR = Thin blood = Bleeding risk. Low INR = Thick blood = Clotting risk.

questions.text: Calcium Channel Blockers avoid grapefruit juice because:
options: Grapefruit inhibits CYP450 enzymes causing toxic drug levels
questions.explanation: Grapefruit Juice inhibits the liver enzyme that breaks down CCBs (and Statins), leading to toxic overdose levels.

questions.text: What is 'Palliative Chemotherapy'?
options: Treatment focused on symptom relief and quality of life, not cure
questions.explanation: Palliative chemo aims to shrink tumors to reduce pain/obstruction, extending quality of life rather than eradicating the disease.

questions.text: Immunosuppressants make patients vulnerable to 'Opportunistic Infections', which are:
options: Infections caused by common organisms that usually don't harm healthy people
questions.explanation: Organisms like Candida or CMV that a healthy immune system ignores become deadly when suppressed.

questions.text: The onset of NPH insulin peak is typically:
questions.explanation: NPH peaks mid-day (6-12 hours). This is when the risk of hypoglycemia is highest if they haven't eaten lunch.

questions.text: Sublingual Nitroglycerin is given for Chest Pain. It works by:
options: Systemic Vasodilation (reducing Preload) and Coronary Artery Dilation
questions.explanation: Nitro causes massive vasodilation. It drops blood pressure (reducing heart work) and opens heart arteries.

questions.text: Which organ is primarily responsible for Drug Excretion?
questions.explanation: The Kidneys filter blood and excrete drug metabolites. Renal failure = Drug Toxicity.

questions.text: Which organ is primarily responsible for Drug Metabolism?
questions.explanation: The Liver is the chemical processing plant (Cytochrome P450 system).

questions.text: What does 'Teratogenic' mean?
questions.explanation: Teratogens cause malformations in a developing fetus (e.g., ACE inhibitors, Retinoids, Thalidomide).

questions.text: Why are 'Statins' given at night?
options: Cholesterol synthesis by the liver is highest at night
questions.explanation: The liver makes the most cholesterol while we sleep, so HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors work best then.

title: Pharmacological Therapies Part 1
description: Medication Administration, CV, Respiratory, Endocrine, Neuro Drugs

questions.text: Nitroglycerin is given for chest pain. Which side effect is most common?
questions.explanation: Headache is a common side effect due to vasodilation. Nitroglycerin causes hypotension and tachycardia, not hypertension or bradycardia.

questions.text: A client is taking warfarin (Coumadin). Which lab value should the nurse monitor?
questions.explanation: PT/INR monitors warfarin effectiveness. Therapeutic INR is typically 2-3. aPTT monitors heparin.

questions.text: Which medication requires checking apical pulse before administration?
questions.explanation: Check apical pulse before digoxin. Hold if pulse <60 bpm (adult) due to risk of bradycardia and heart block.

questions.text: A client taking furosemide (Lasix) should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes potassium loss. Monitor for hypokalemia; encourage potassium-rich foods or supplements.

questions.text: Which drug suffix indicates a beta blocker?
questions.explanation: -lol = beta blockers (metoprolol, atenolol). -pril = ACE inhibitors, -statin = cholesterol drugs, -dipine = calcium channel blockers.

questions.text: A client on lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) develops a dry cough. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Dry cough is a common side effect of ACE inhibitors. Notify provider; client may need to switch to an ARB (angiotensin receptor blocker).

questions.text: What are the 'Five Rights' of medication administration?
options: Right patient, drug, dose, route, time
Right patient, drug, doctor, route, time
Right patient, drug, dose, room, time
Right patient, drug, dose, route, documentation
questions.explanation: Five Rights: Right patient, drug, dose, route, and time. Some add: right documentation, right reason, right to refuse.

questions.text: A client is prescribed albuterol inhaler. What is the primary action?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist bronchodilator for quick relief of bronchospasm. Corticosteroids reduce inflammation.

questions.text: Which insulin has the fastest onset?
questions.explanation: Rapid-acting insulins (lispro, aspart) have onset in 10-15 minutes. Give immediately before meals. Regular onset is 30-60 min.

questions.text: A client taking levothyroxine (Synthroid) for hypothyroidism should take it:
options: On an empty stomach in the morning
questions.explanation: Take levothyroxine on empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast for best absorption. Avoid taking with calcium or iron.

questions.text: Which is a sign of digoxin toxicity?
questions.explanation: Digoxin toxicity signs: visual changes (halos), nausea, bradycardia, arrhythmias. Therapeutic level: 0.5-2 ng/mL.

questions.text: A client is prescribed metoprolol (beta blocker). Which condition is a contraindication?
questions.explanation: Beta blockers can cause bronchoconstriction and are contraindicated in asthma/COPD. Also avoid in heart block and bradycardia.

questions.text: Which medication should be given with food to reduce GI upset?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs like ibuprofen should be taken with food to reduce GI irritation and ulcer risk.

questions.text: A client using an inhaled corticosteroid should:
options: Use it for acute asthma attacks
questions.explanation: Rinse mouth after inhaled corticosteroids to prevent oral thrush. Use bronchodilator first, then corticosteroid. Not for acute attacks.

questions.text: What is the antidote for heparin overdose?
questions.explanation: Protamine sulfate reverses heparin. Vitamin K reverses warfarin. Naloxone reverses opioids. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines.

questions.text: A client is prescribed NPH insulin. When is the peak action?
questions.explanation: NPH (intermediate-acting) peaks at 4-12 hours. Monitor for hypoglycemia during peak. Glargine/detemir have no peak.

questions.text: Which lab value indicates therapeutic effect of warfarin?
questions.explanation: Therapeutic INR for warfarin is 2-3 (may be 2.5-3.5 for mechanical heart valves). INR 0.8-1.2 is normal/subtherapeutic.

questions.text: A client taking amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) should avoid:
questions.explanation: Grapefruit juice increases calcium channel blocker levels, causing excessive hypotension. Avoid grapefruit with many medications.

questions.text: Which route has the fastest absorption?
questions.explanation: IV has immediate onset (fastest). Then: IM, SubQ, oral (slowest). Sublingual and inhalation are also rapid.

questions.text: A client is prescribed prednisone (corticosteroid). Which side effect should the nurse monitor?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids cause hyperglycemia, hypertension, weight gain, immunosuppression, and osteoporosis with long-term use.

questions.text: Which medication requires a peak and trough level?
questions.explanation: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin) require peak/trough monitoring due to narrow therapeutic window and nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity risk.

questions.text: A client is prescribed metformin for diabetes. What is a serious side effect?
questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis is a rare but serious side effect of metformin. Hold before contrast dye procedures. Metformin alone doesn't cause hypoglycemia.

questions.text: Which drug class ends in '-pril'?
questions.explanation: -pril = ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril). Monitor for dry cough, hyperkalemia, and angioedema.

questions.text: A client is prescribed spironolactone (Aldactone). The nurse should monitor for:
questions.explanation: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic. Monitor for hyperkalemia. Avoid potassium supplements and salt substitutes.

questions.text: What is the antidote for warfarin overdose?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K (phytonadione) reverses warfarin. IV for severe bleeding, PO for minor. Protamine reverses heparin.

questions.text: A client is prescribed phenytoin (Dilantin) for seizures. Which side effect is common?
questions.explanation: Phenytoin causes gingival hyperplasia (gum overgrowth). Encourage good oral hygiene. Also causes hirsutism and ataxia.

questions.text: Which insulin is long-acting with no peak?
questions.explanation: Glargine and detemir are long-acting (24 hours) with no peak. Provide basal coverage. Cannot be mixed with other insulins.

questions.text: A client taking hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) should increase intake of:
questions.explanation: HCTZ is a thiazide diuretic causing potassium loss. Encourage potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes).

questions.text: Which medication should NOT be crushed?
questions.explanation: Never crush enteric-coated or sustained-release medications. Crushing destroys the coating and alters absorption.

questions.text: A client is prescribed nitroglycerin sublingual. How should it be taken?
questions.explanation: Sublingual nitroglycerin dissolves under tongue for rapid absorption. Can repeat every 5 minutes x3. Call 911 if pain persists.

questions.text: Which adverse effect is associated with ACE inhibitors?
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors can cause hyperkalemia by reducing aldosterone. Also cause dry cough and angioedema (rare but serious).

questions.text: A client is prescribed albuterol and fluticasone inhalers. Which should be used first?
questions.explanation: Use bronchodilator first to open airways, then corticosteroid for better penetration. Wait 1-2 minutes between inhalers.

questions.text: Which medication requires monitoring of liver function tests?
questions.explanation: Acetaminophen is hepatotoxic in high doses or chronic use. Monitor LFTs. Maximum dose: 4g/day (3g/day for elderly or liver disease).

questions.text: A client is prescribed enoxaparin (Lovenox). What is the route of administration?
questions.explanation: Enoxaparin (low molecular weight heparin) is given subcutaneously. Do not aspirate or massage injection site. Rotate sites.

questions.text: Which statement indicates a client understands insulin teaching?
options: I'll store insulin in the freezer.
I'll reuse needles to save money.
I'll skip doses if I'm not eating.
questions.explanation: Rotate injection sites to prevent lipodystrophy. Don't freeze insulin. Use new needles. Don't skip long-acting insulin even if not eating.

questions.text: A client taking atorvastatin (statin) reports muscle pain. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Muscle pain may indicate rhabdomyolysis (rare but serious). Notify provider. Monitor CK levels. May need to discontinue statin.

questions.text: Which medication should be taken 30 minutes before breakfast?
questions.explanation: Alendronate (bisphosphonate) taken 30-60 min before breakfast with full glass of water. Remain upright 30 min to prevent esophageal irritation.

questions.text: A client is prescribed morphine. Which vital sign is priority to monitor?
questions.explanation: Opioids cause respiratory depression. Monitor respiratory rate; hold if <12/min. Have naloxone (Narcan) available for overdose.

questions.text: Which drug class ends in '-dipine'?
questions.explanation: -dipine = calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine). Cause vasodilation, reduce BP. Monitor for peripheral edema, headache.

questions.text: A client is prescribed regular insulin and NPH insulin. Which should be drawn up first?
questions.explanation: Draw up clear (regular) before cloudy (NPH). Mnemonic: 'Clear before cloudy' or 'RN' (Regular before NPH).

title: Pharmacological Therapies Part 2
description: Pain Management, GI, Psychiatric, Geriatric Pharmacology

questions.text: When administering lithium, the nurse monitors for toxicity. Early signs include:
questions.explanation: Early lithium toxicity: fine tremors, nausea, polyuria. Advanced toxicity: coarse tremors, vomiting, confusion, seizures. Therapeutic level: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L.

questions.text: What is the antidote for opioid overdose?
questions.explanation: Naloxone reverses opioid effects including respiratory depression. May need repeated doses. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines.

questions.text: A client taking an SSRI (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor) should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome symptoms: agitation, confusion, tremor, hyperthermia, muscle rigidity. Can occur with SSRI overdose or drug interactions.

questions.text: Which pain scale is appropriate for a 4-year-old child?
questions.explanation: FACES scale (Wong-Baker) is appropriate for children 3+ years. Shows faces from happy to crying. Numeric scales work for ages 8+.

questions.text: A client is prescribed morphine PCA (patient-controlled analgesia). What is the priority assessment?
questions.explanation: Monitor respiratory rate closely with opioids. Hold if <12/min. Also assess sedation level, pain relief, and for side effects.

questions.text: Which medication is used to treat extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) from antipsychotics?
questions.explanation: Benztropine (anticholinergic) treats EPS (dystonia, akathisia, pseudoparkinsonism) caused by typical antipsychotics.

questions.text: A client taking omeprazole (proton pump inhibitor) for GERD should take it:
questions.explanation: PPIs work best when taken 30 minutes before first meal of the day on empty stomach for maximum acid suppression.

questions.text: Which is a common side effect of opioid analgesics?
questions.explanation: Opioids commonly cause constipation. Prevent with stool softeners, fluids, fiber. Also cause nausea, sedation, respiratory depression.

questions.text: A client is prescribed lorazepam (Ativan) for anxiety. Which statement indicates understanding?
options: I can drink alcohol while taking this.
I should avoid driving until I know how this affects me.
I can stop taking this abruptly.
questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines cause sedation. Avoid alcohol and driving. Don't stop abruptly (risk of seizures). Can be habit-forming.

questions.text: Which antiemetic is contraindicated in Parkinson's disease?
questions.explanation: Metoclopramide blocks dopamine and can worsen Parkinson's symptoms. Ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) is safe alternative.

questions.text: What is the maximum daily dose of acetaminophen for adults?
questions.explanation: Maximum acetaminophen: 4g/day for healthy adults, 3g/day for elderly or liver disease. Exceeding causes hepatotoxicity.

questions.text: A client taking haloperidol (typical antipsychotic) develops muscle rigidity, fever, and altered mental status. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is life-threatening: muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, altered consciousness, autonomic instability. Stop drug immediately.

questions.text: Which laxative works by drawing water into the intestine?
questions.explanation: Osmotic laxatives (PEG, lactulose, magnesium citrate) draw water into bowel. Gentler than stimulant laxatives.

questions.text: A geriatric client is at higher risk for medication toxicity due to:
questions.explanation: Elderly have decreased kidney/liver function, increased body fat, decreased lean mass. Start low, go slow with dosing.

questions.text: Which SSRI has the longest half-life?
questions.explanation: Fluoxetine has longest half-life (4-6 days). Can discontinue without tapering. Other SSRIs require gradual taper to avoid withdrawal.

questions.text: A client is prescribed fentanyl patch for chronic pain. When does it reach peak effect?
questions.explanation: Fentanyl patch takes 12-24 hours to reach peak. Change every 72 hours. Dispose properly (fold sticky sides together).

questions.text: Which medication requires monitoring for tardive dyskinesia?
questions.explanation: Tardive dyskinesia (involuntary movements) occurs with long-term antipsychotic use, especially typical antipsychotics. May be irreversible.

questions.text: A client taking an MAOI (monoamine oxidase inhibitor) should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid tyramine-rich foods with MAOIs (aged cheese, wine, cured meats) - can cause hypertensive crisis. Rarely used due to dietary restrictions.

questions.text: Which pain medication is contraindicated in clients with peptic ulcer disease?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) increase ulcer risk and GI bleeding. Acetaminophen is safer for clients with ulcers.

questions.text: A client is prescribed ondansetron (Zofran) for chemotherapy-induced nausea. When should it be given?
questions.explanation: Give antiemetics 30 minutes before chemotherapy for prevention. More effective than treating nausea after it occurs.

questions.text: Which statement about tramadol is correct?
questions.explanation: Tramadol is a weak opioid that lowers seizure threshold. Avoid in seizure disorders. Has lower respiratory depression risk than morphine.

questions.text: A client taking clozapine (atypical antipsychotic) requires monitoring of:
questions.explanation: Clozapine requires WBC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk), blood glucose (diabetes risk), and LFTs. Most effective but highest risk antipsychotic.

questions.text: Which medication is used for opioid-induced constipation?
questions.explanation: Stool softeners (docusate) and stimulant laxatives prevent/treat opioid-induced constipation. Start prophylactically with opioid therapy.

questions.text: A client is prescribed gabapentin for neuropathic pain. Which statement is correct?
options: It is an anticonvulsant used for nerve pain
questions.explanation: Gabapentin is an anticonvulsant effective for neuropathic pain (diabetic neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia). Taper gradually to discontinue.

questions.text: Which antacid should be avoided in clients with kidney disease?
questions.explanation: Magnesium-containing antacids can cause hypermagnesemia in kidney disease. Aluminum antacids can cause constipation.

questions.text: A client taking duloxetine (Cymbalta) for depression and pain should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine) can increase blood pressure. Monitor BP regularly. Also effective for neuropathic pain and fibromyalgia.

questions.text: Which medication is first-line for acute migraine?
questions.explanation: Triptans (sumatriptan) are first-line for acute migraine. Contraindicated in coronary artery disease. NSAIDs are alternative.

questions.text: A geriatric client should avoid which medication class due to fall risk?
questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines increase fall risk in elderly due to sedation and impaired coordination. Use cautiously or avoid. Consider alternatives.

questions.text: Which is a sign of anticholinergic toxicity?
options: Dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention
questions.explanation: Anticholinergic toxicity: 'Dry as a bone, blind as a bat, red as a beet, hot as a hare, mad as a hatter.' Causes dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, hyperthermia, confusion.

questions.text: A client is prescribed bisacodyl (Dulcolax). What is the mechanism of action?
questions.explanation: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative that increases intestinal motility. Works in 6-12 hours orally, 15-60 min rectally. Don't use long-term.

questions.text: Which pain assessment tool is appropriate for a nonverbal client?
questions.explanation: FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) assesses pain in nonverbal clients. Also use behavioral indicators: grimacing, guarding, restlessness.

questions.text: A client taking risperidone (atypical antipsychotic) should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Atypical antipsychotics cause weight gain, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia (metabolic syndrome). Monitor weight, glucose, lipids regularly.

questions.text: Which medication is used to reverse benzodiazepine overdose?
questions.explanation: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines. Use cautiously - can precipitate seizures in chronic users. Naloxone reverses opioids.

questions.text: A client is prescribed sucralfate (Carafate) for peptic ulcer. When should it be taken?
questions.explanation: Sucralfate forms protective barrier over ulcer. Take 1 hour before meals and at bedtime on empty stomach. Don't take with antacids.

questions.text: Which is a Beers Criteria medication to avoid in elderly?
questions.explanation: Beers Criteria lists potentially inappropriate medications for elderly. Diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) causes confusion, falls, urinary retention. Avoid in elderly.

title: Respiratory & Neurological - Part A
description: COPD, Asthma, Pneumonia, Delirium, Dementia, Stroke, Seizures

questions.text: A patient with COPD has chronic bronchitis. What is the primary pathophysiological mechanism?
options: Chronic inflammation with excessive mucus production
questions.explanation: Chronic bronchitis is fundamentally an inflammatory issue with excessive mucus production and impaired ciliary function, causing airway obstruction.

questions.text: What distinguishes emphysema from chronic bronchitis in COPD?
options: Emphysema involves irreversible destruction of alveoli and loss of elastic recoil
questions.explanation: Emphysema is pure structural failure with irreversible destruction of alveoli, causing loss of elastic recoil and difficulty exhaling.

questions.text: A COPD patient has difficulty exhaling fully. What complication is most likely?
options: Air trapping and hypercapnia leading to respiratory acidosis
questions.explanation: Emphysema causes difficulty exhaling, leading to air trapping, CO2 retention (hypercapnia), and eventually respiratory acidosis.

questions.text: What is the FEV1/FVC ratio used to assess in COPD?
questions.explanation: The FEV1/FVC ratio measures how much air can be forced out in one second compared to total exhaled volume, indicating severity of obstruction.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY disease modifier for COPD?
questions.explanation: Smoking cessation is the number one intervention that can actually modify disease progression in COPD.

questions.text: Status asthmaticus is defined as:
options: Mild asthma requiring occasional inhaler use
Severe acute asthma attack not responding to conventional therapy
questions.explanation: Status asthmaticus is a life-threatening severe acute attack that does not respond to rescue inhalers or conventional treatment.

questions.text: What is pulsus paradoxus and why is it significant in status asthmaticus?
options: Drop in systolic BP >10 mmHg during inspiration, indicating severe obstruction
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus is a drop in systolic BP >10 mmHg during inspiration, caused by increased intrathoracic pressure compressing the heart.

questions.text: What clinical sign indicates imminent respiratory failure in status asthmaticus?
options: Muscle fatigue, declining mental status, or cardiac arrest
questions.explanation: Muscle fatigue from the work of breathing, altered mental status, or cardiac arrest signal imminent respiratory failure requiring ventilation.

questions.text: What makes aspiration pneumonia unique compared to other pneumonias?
options: It starts as a chemical burn if pH <2.5, causing immediate lung tissue injury
It only affects the upper airways
It's easily treated with antibiotics alone
questions.explanation: Aspiration of acidic stomach contents (pH <2.5) causes immediate chemical injury to lung tissue before bacterial infection develops.

questions.text: What is the primary nursing intervention to prevent respiratory failure?
options: Turn, position, ambulate patients; encourage deep breathing and coughing
questions.explanation: Turning, positioning, ambulation, and encouraging deep breathing/coughing prevent fluid pooling and maintain airway clearance.

questions.text: What is the key difference between delirium and dementia?
options: Delirium is acute, sudden, fluctuating, and usually reversible
questions.explanation: Delirium is acute, sudden, and fluctuating with reversible causes (UTI, drugs, electrolytes). Dementia is chronic and progressive.

questions.text: What is the appropriate intervention for delirium?
options: Identify and correct the underlying cause (UTI, electrolytes, drugs)
questions.explanation: The priority is identifying and treating the underlying cause. Sedatives can worsen confusion, especially in elderly patients.

questions.text: Why should sedating medications be avoided in elderly patients with delirium?
options: They mask the metabolic chaos without fixing it and can worsen confusion
questions.explanation: Sedatives like Trazodone or Lorazepam mask symptoms without treating the cause and often worsen confusion in older adults.

questions.text: What percentage of strokes are ischemic versus hemorrhagic?
questions.explanation: The 80/20 rule: 80% of strokes are ischemic (blockage), while 20% are hemorrhagic (ruptured artery).

questions.text: Why is hemorrhagic stroke immediately dangerous?
options: Bleeding adds volume to the closed skull, rapidly increasing intracranial pressure
questions.explanation: Hemorrhagic stroke adds blood volume to the closed skull, causing rapid ICP increase and brain compression.

questions.text: Right hemisphere stroke causes deficits on which side of the body?
questions.explanation: Right hemisphere damage causes left-sided paralysis and sensory deficits, plus issues with spatial judgment and visual neglect.

questions.text: Left hemisphere stroke critically affects which functions?
questions.explanation: Left hemisphere damage affects the right side of the body and critically compromises speech, language, and mathematical abilities.

questions.text: What is a TIA and why is it significant?
options: A minor stroke with no consequences
Transient ischemic attack - symptoms resolve but signal extremely high risk for major stroke
questions.explanation: TIA symptoms resolve completely but serve as a critical warning sign of imminent major stroke risk.

questions.text: What is the time window for administering tPA in ischemic stroke?
questions.explanation: tPA (tissue plasminogen activator) must be given within 3-4.5 hours of symptom onset to be effective. Time is brain!

questions.text: Why is tPA contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke?
options: It's a clot-buster that would worsen active bleeding in the brain
questions.explanation: tPA breaks down clots. Giving it to someone with active brain bleeding would be fatal by preventing clot formation.

questions.text: What is the primary intervention for hemorrhagic stroke?
options: Strict blood pressure control to stop bleeding
questions.explanation: Hemorrhagic stroke requires strict BP control to stop bleeding, plus ICP management with positioning and hypertonic fluids.

questions.text: Why are hypertonic fluids (mannitol, hypertonic saline) used for increased ICP?
options: To create an osmotic gradient that pulls fluid out of swollen brain tissue
questions.explanation: Hypertonic fluids create an osmotic gradient to pull fluid from the brain compartment, reducing swelling without dropping systemic BP.

questions.text: What distinguishes partial seizures from generalized seizures?
options: Partial seizures start in one localized spot; generalized involve both hemispheres
Partial seizures only occur in children
questions.explanation: Partial seizures originate in one localized area, while generalized seizures (like tonic-clonic) involve both brain hemispheres.

questions.text: Status epilepticus is defined as:
options: Seizure lasting >5 minutes OR recurrent seizures without regaining consciousness
questions.explanation: Status epilepticus is a seizure >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness - a medical emergency.

questions.text: Why is status epilepticus life-threatening?
options: Sustained muscle activity burns through oxygen, causing hypoxia and potential respiratory failure
questions.explanation: Uncontrolled muscle activity depletes oxygen, leading to hypoxia, exhaustion, and potentially fatal respiratory failure.

questions.text: What is the first-line medication for status epilepticus?
questions.explanation: Rapid-acting benzodiazepines like lorazepam are given every 5 minutes to stop the electrical storm and prevent respiratory failure.

questions.text: A patient with COPD is prescribed albuterol. What is its mechanism of action?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is a bronchodilator that relaxes airway smooth muscle to improve airflow in COPD and asthma.

questions.text: What surgical option removes large non-functional air sacs in severe emphysema?
questions.explanation: Bullectomy removes giant non-functional air sacs (bullae) in severe emphysema to improve lung function.

questions.text: What type of dementia is characterized by fluctuating symptoms and visual hallucinations?
questions.explanation: Dementia with Lewy bodies presents with fluctuating cognition and complex visual hallucinations.

questions.text: Vascular dementia is caused by:
questions.explanation: Vascular dementia results from multiple small strokes that cumulatively damage brain tissue over time.

questions.text: What is the most common type of dementia?
questions.explanation: Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia, characterized by progressive cognitive decline.

questions.text: A patient shows sternal and intercostal retractions during breathing. This indicates:
options: Severe respiratory distress with airway obstruction
questions.explanation: Retractions (skin sucking in between ribs) indicate the patient is using accessory muscles due to severe respiratory distress.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an incentive spirometer?
options: To provide visual feedback for deep breathing exercises
questions.explanation: An incentive spirometer provides visual feedback to encourage deep breathing and prevent atelectasis.

questions.text: CPAP and BiPAP are examples of:
questions.explanation: CPAP and BiPAP are non-invasive methods that use positive pressure to physically force the lungs open.

questions.text: What is the primary goal when treating any respiratory emergency?
options: Identify and treat the underlying cause
questions.explanation: The core principle is to immediately identify and treat the underlying cause, whether infection, obstruction, or other pathology.

title: Endocrine & Critical Care - Part B
description: Diabetes, DKA, HHNS, Critical Assessment, Interventions

questions.text: What is the fundamental problem in diabetes mellitus?
options: Problem with insulin - the key that lets glucose into cells
questions.explanation: Diabetes is fundamentally a problem with insulin, which acts as the key allowing glucose to enter cells for fuel.

questions.text: Type 1 diabetes is characterized by:
options: Absolute insulin deficiency due to destroyed pancreatic beta cells
questions.explanation: Type 1 is an absolute insulin deficiency from destroyed beta cells, requiring lifelong exogenous insulin.

questions.text: Type 2 diabetes initially involves:
options: Insulin resistance first, then inadequate production later
questions.explanation: Type 2 starts with insulin resistance, then progresses to inadequate insulin production. Usually managed with diet, exercise, and oral medications.

questions.text: What are the 'three polys' of diabetes?
questions.explanation: The classic signs are polyuria (frequent urination), polydipsia (excessive thirst), and polyphagia (excessive hunger).

questions.text: DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis) is typically a complication of which type of diabetes?
questions.explanation: DKA typically occurs in Type 1 diabetes due to absolute lack of insulin, causing the body to break down fat for fuel.

questions.text: In DKA, why does the body break down fat?
options: Because cells can't get glucose without insulin, so the body thinks it's starving
questions.explanation: Without insulin, cells can't access glucose. The body perceives starvation and breaks down fat for alternative fuel.

questions.text: What are ketones and why are they dangerous?
options: Highly acidic byproducts of fat breakdown that cause metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: Ketones are acidic byproducts of fat metabolism. When they accumulate, they overwhelm buffers and cause severe metabolic acidosis.

questions.text: What is Kussmaul breathing and what does it indicate?
options: Deep, labored, rapid breaths attempting to blow off CO2 and raise pH
questions.explanation: Kussmaul respirations are deep, rapid breaths - the body's attempt to compensate for acidosis by exhaling CO2 (an acid).

questions.text: What causes the fruity breath odor in DKA?
questions.explanation: The fruity smell is acetone, a volatile ketone being exhaled through the lungs.

questions.text: What lab values confirm DKA?
questions.explanation: DKA is confirmed by glucose >250 mg/dL, pH <7.3, and presence of ketones in blood or urine.

questions.text: What is the FIRST priority in treating DKA?
options: Aggressive fluid resuscitation to correct dehydration
questions.explanation: Fluids first! Must correct severe dehydration and poor perfusion before starting insulin therapy.

questions.text: Why is it dangerous to give insulin before addressing potassium in DKA?
options: Insulin drives potassium into cells, potentially causing fatal hypokalemia
questions.explanation: Insulin shifts potassium into cells. If serum potassium is already low, insulin can cause dangerous or fatal hypokalemia.

questions.text: What are the three priorities in DKA treatment, in order?
questions.explanation: The order is critical: 1) Fluids to correct dehydration, 2) IV insulin to stop ketone production, 3) Potassium replacement.

questions.text: HHNS (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic non-ketotic syndrome) typically occurs in which type of diabetes?
questions.explanation: HHNS typically occurs in Type 2 diabetes, where there's enough insulin to prevent ketone formation but not enough to control glucose.

questions.text: What is the key difference between DKA and HHNS?
questions.explanation: HHNS shares high glucose and dehydration with DKA, but lacks ketones and acidosis because some insulin is present.

questions.text: In HHNS, glucose levels can reach:
questions.explanation: HHNS can have astronomical glucose levels, sometimes exceeding 600 mg/dL, causing profound dehydration.

questions.text: What is the primary intervention focus in HHNS?
options: Massive fluid resuscitation first, then controlled insulin
questions.explanation: HHNS is primarily a dehydration crisis. Treatment focuses on aggressive fluid replacement, then controlled insulin administration.

questions.text: Why is respiratory failure described as a 'common lethal endpoint' for many crises?
options: Multiple system failures (seizures, DKA, COPD) all can lead to respiratory collapse
questions.explanation: Whether from status epilepticus exhaustion, DKA compensation, or COPD structural failure, many crises end in respiratory failure.

questions.text: A patient in DKA shows signs of volume depletion. What causes this?
options: Osmotic pull of high glucose in urine causing massive fluid loss
questions.explanation: High glucose in urine creates osmotic pull, dragging water out and causing severe dehydration, hypotension, and tachycardia.

questions.text: What connects the respiratory and endocrine systems in DKA?
options: Kussmaul breathing is the respiratory system compensating for metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: The respiratory system attempts to compensate for DKA's metabolic acidosis through Kussmaul breathing to blow off CO2.

questions.text: What assessment finding would indicate successful DKA treatment?
options: Normalizing pH, decreasing glucose, improved mental status
questions.explanation: Successful treatment shows normalizing pH, decreasing glucose levels, clearing ketones, and improved mental status.

questions.text: Why must glucose be monitored closely during DKA treatment?
options: To prevent hypoglycemia as insulin works and to adjust treatment
questions.explanation: As insulin lowers glucose, close monitoring prevents hypoglycemia and guides treatment adjustments.

questions.text: What role does the neurological system play in recognizing metabolic crises?
options: Altered mental status signals severe metabolic derangement
questions.explanation: Changes in mental status (confusion, lethargy, coma) are critical signs of severe metabolic disturbance in DKA/HHNS.

questions.text: A patient with diabetes presents with confusion. What should be assessed first?
questions.explanation: In diabetic patients with altered mental status, immediately check blood glucose to rule out hypo- or hyperglycemia.

questions.text: What makes the interconnection of respiratory, neurological, and endocrine systems clinically significant?
options: Failure in one system rapidly affects the others, creating cascading emergencies
questions.explanation: These systems are profoundly interconnected - failure in one (e.g., DKA) immediately impacts others (respiratory compensation, altered mental status).

questions.text: What is the clinical significance of recognizing Kussmaul breathing?
options: It instantly signals severe metabolic acidosis requiring immediate intervention
It means the patient is anxious
questions.explanation: Kussmaul breathing is a critical red flag instantly indicating severe acidosis (likely DKA) requiring emergency treatment.

questions.text: Why is 'time is brain' emphasized in stroke care?
options: Every minute without treatment causes more irreversible brain damage
questions.explanation: Brain tissue dies rapidly without blood flow. Every minute of delay causes more permanent damage, making rapid intervention critical.

questions.text: What principle guides all critical care interventions discussed?
options: Identify and treat the underlying cause immediately
questions.explanation: The core principle across all emergencies is to immediately identify and treat the underlying cause, not just mask symptoms.

questions.text: How do assessment flags like pulsus paradoxus or Kussmaul breathing help clinicians?
options: They instantly reveal underlying pathology and dictate immediate life-saving interventions
questions.explanation: These clinical flags instantly clue you into the underlying crisis and tell you exactly what intervention is needed to save the patient.

questions.text: What makes understanding system interconnections essential for nursing practice?
options: It transforms facts into a clinical roadmap for recognizing and treating cascading emergencies
questions.explanation: Understanding how systems interact transforms isolated facts into an actionable clinical roadmap for managing complex, multi-system emergencies.

title: Respiratory, Neurological & Endocrine
description: COPD, Asthma, Stroke, Seizures, Diabetes, Thyroid, ABGs

questions.text: A client with COPD should be positioned:
questions.explanation: High Fowler's (sitting upright) maximizes lung expansion and eases breathing in COPD. Also use pursed-lip breathing.

questions.text: What oxygen level is appropriate for a client with COPD?
questions.explanation: COPD clients need low-flow oxygen (1-3 L/min) to maintain hypoxic drive. High oxygen can suppress respiratory drive.

questions.text: A client having a tonic-clonic seizure. What is the priority action?
options: Protect from injury and turn to side
questions.explanation: Protect from injury, turn to side (prevents aspiration), loosen clothing. Never restrain or put anything in mouth. Time the seizure.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates respiratory acidosis?
questions.explanation: Respiratory acidosis: pH <7.35, PaCO2 >45 (retaining CO2). Causes: COPD, hypoventilation, respiratory depression.

questions.text: A client with diabetes has blood glucose of 45 mg/dL. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL): Give 15g simple carbs (juice, glucose tablets), recheck in 15 min. If unconscious, give glucagon or D50W IV.

questions.text: A client with asthma is wheezing. Which medication provides quickest relief?
questions.explanation: Albuterol (beta-2 agonist) provides quick relief of bronchospasm. Corticosteroids reduce inflammation but take hours/days to work.

questions.text: Which sign indicates increased intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: Cushing's triad (late ICP sign): Widening pulse pressure, bradycardia, irregular respirations. Also: decreased LOC, headache, vomiting, dilated pupils.

questions.text: A client with hypothyroidism will have:
questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism: Weight gain, bradycardia, fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, depression. Treat with levothyroxine.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates metabolic alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Metabolic alkalosis: pH >7.45, HCO3 >26. Causes: vomiting, NG suction, excess antacids, diuretics.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia should:
options: Cough and deep breathe every 2 hours
questions.explanation: Encourage coughing/deep breathing, incentive spirometer, fluids (thins secretions), ambulation. Monitor for respiratory distress.

questions.text: A client post-stroke has left-sided weakness. Where should the nurse stand during ambulation?
questions.explanation: Stand on the weaker side to provide support and prevent falls. Use gait belt for safety.

questions.text: Which finding indicates DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis)?
options: Blood glucose 600 mg/dL with fruity breath
questions.explanation: DKA: Hyperglycemia (>250), ketones, fruity breath, Kussmaul respirations, dehydration. Treat with IV insulin and fluids.

questions.text: A client with COPD is receiving oxygen. Which finding is concerning?
questions.explanation: Respiratory rate <12 indicates respiratory depression from too much oxygen (suppressed hypoxic drive). Reduce oxygen, stimulate breathing.

questions.text: Which medication prevents seizures?
questions.explanation: Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, valproic acid, carbamazepine) prevent seizures. Monitor drug levels, don't stop abruptly.

questions.text: A client with hyperthyroidism will have:
questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease): Weight loss, tachycardia, heat intolerance, exophthalmos, tremors, anxiety. Treat with antithyroid drugs or radioactive iodine.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates respiratory alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Respiratory alkalosis: pH >7.45, PaCO2 <35 (blowing off CO2). Causes: hyperventilation, anxiety, pain, fever.

questions.text: A client with asthma should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid asthma triggers: smoke, allergens, cold air, exercise (without pre-medication). Use peak flow meter to monitor.

questions.text: A client post-stroke has difficulty swallowing. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Dysphagia increases aspiration risk. Keep NPO until swallow study. Elevate HOB 30-90°, thicken liquids if cleared.

questions.text: Which sign indicates hypoglycemia?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia: Shakiness, sweating, tachycardia, confusion, hunger. Treat immediately with 15g simple carbs. Hyperglycemia: polyuria, polydipsia, dry skin.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Metabolic acidosis: pH <7.35, HCO3 <22. Causes: DKA, renal failure, diarrhea, lactic acidosis.

questions.text: A client with COPD should use which breathing technique?
questions.explanation: Pursed-lip breathing: Inhale through nose, exhale slowly through pursed lips. Prevents airway collapse, improves gas exchange.

questions.text: A client with increased ICP should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid Valsalva, hip flexion, coughing (increases ICP). Elevate HOB 30°, keep head midline, prevent constipation, minimize stimulation.

questions.text: Which is a sign of thyroid storm?
questions.explanation: Thyroid storm (hyperthyroid crisis): Severe tachycardia, hyperthermia, agitation, delirium. Life-threatening. Give antithyroid drugs, beta blockers, cooling measures.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia has rusty-colored sputum. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Rusty/blood-tinged sputum suggests bacterial pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae). Green sputum = Pseudomonas. Treat with antibiotics.

questions.text: A client post-seizure is confused and sleepy. This is called:
questions.explanation: Postictal state: Confusion, drowsiness, headache after seizure. Provide safety, reorient, allow rest. Document seizure characteristics.

questions.text: Which insulin should be given for DKA?
questions.explanation: DKA requires regular insulin IV drip (only insulin that can be given IV). Also give IV fluids, monitor potassium, treat underlying cause.

questions.text: A client with asthma has a peak flow reading in the red zone. This means:
questions.explanation: Peak flow zones: Green (80-100% = good), Yellow (50-80% = caution), Red (<50% = emergency). Red zone requires immediate treatment.

questions.text: Which position is best for a client with increased ICP?
questions.explanation: Elevate HOB 30° to promote venous drainage and reduce ICP. Keep head midline (avoid neck flexion/rotation).

questions.text: A client with diabetes is NPO for surgery. What should the nurse do with morning insulin?
options: Give half dose or per protocol
questions.explanation: NPO: Usually give half dose long-acting insulin or per facility protocol. Monitor glucose closely. Don't give rapid-acting insulin without food.

questions.text: Which finding indicates status epilepticus?
questions.explanation: Status epilepticus: Continuous seizure >5 min or repeated seizures without regaining consciousness. Medical emergency. Give IV benzodiazepines.

questions.text: A client with COPD should receive which vaccine?
questions.explanation: COPD clients need pneumococcal vaccine and annual flu vaccine to prevent respiratory infections.

questions.text: Which cranial nerve is assessed by having the client smile and frown?
questions.explanation: CN VII (Facial) controls facial expressions. Bell's palsy affects CN VII causing facial drooping. Stroke can also cause facial asymmetry.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hyperglycemia?
questions.explanation: Hyperglycemia: 3 P's (Polyuria, Polydipsia, Polyphagia), blurred vision, fatigue, dry skin. Chronic hyperglycemia causes complications.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia has decreased breath sounds in right lower lobe. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Decreased/absent breath sounds indicate consolidation, fluid, or collapsed lung. Pneumonia causes consolidation. Also assess for dullness to percussion.

questions.text: Which is the first step in ABG interpretation?
questions.explanation: ABG steps: 1) pH (acidosis <7.35 or alkalosis >7.45), 2) PaCO2 (respiratory), 3) HCO3 (metabolic), 4) Determine if compensated.

questions.text: A client with stroke has aphasia. This means:
questions.explanation: Aphasia: Language impairment. Expressive = can't speak, Receptive = can't understand, Global = both. Dysphagia = swallowing difficulty.

questions.text: Which medication treats hyperthyroidism?
questions.explanation: Antithyroid drugs (methimazole, PTU) treat hyperthyroidism. Also: radioactive iodine or thyroidectomy. Levothyroxine treats hypothyroidism.

questions.text: A client with asthma is using a metered-dose inhaler. What should the nurse teach?
options: Shake inhaler, exhale, inhale slowly while pressing canister
questions.explanation: MDI technique: Shake, exhale fully, press canister while inhaling slowly, hold breath 10 seconds. Use spacer for better delivery.

questions.text: Which is a late sign of increased ICP?
questions.explanation: Early ICP signs: Headache, nausea, restlessness, decreased LOC. Late signs: Cushing's triad (widening pulse pressure, bradycardia, irregular respirations), dilated pupils.

questions.text: A client with diabetes has HbA1c of 9%. This indicates:
questions.explanation: HbA1c goal: <7% for most diabetics. 9% indicates poor control over past 2-3 months. Adjust medications, reinforce diet/exercise.

title: Electrolytes, Burns & Acid-Base
description: Electrolyte Imbalances, ABG Interpretation, Burn Management

questions.text: A client with hypokalemia (K+ 2.8) will have:
questions.explanation: Hypokalemia (<3.5): Flat T waves, muscle weakness, cramping, arrhythmias, constipation. Give potassium supplements/IV cautiously.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates fully compensated metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Fully compensated: pH normal (7.35-7.45), both PaCO2 and HCO3 abnormal. Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation.

questions.text: A client with hyperkalemia (K+ 6.5) should receive:
questions.explanation: Hyperkalemia (>5.0): Calcium gluconate (protects heart), insulin/glucose, kayexalate, dialysis. Monitor ECG for peaked T waves, widened QRS.

questions.text: A burn client needs fluid resuscitation. Which formula is used?
questions.explanation: Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA burned. Give half in first 8 hours, half in next 16 hours. Monitor urine output 30-50 mL/hr.

questions.text: A client with hyponatremia (Na+ 125) will have:
questions.explanation: Hyponatremia (<135): Confusion, lethargy, seizures, muscle cramps. Causes: SIADH, diuretics, water intoxication. Correct slowly to avoid central pontine myelinolysis.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hypocalcemia?
questions.explanation: Hypocalcemia (<8.5): Trousseau's (carpal spasm), Chvostek's (facial twitching), tetany, seizures, prolonged QT. Give calcium IV slowly.

questions.text: A client with DKA has which acid-base imbalance?
questions.explanation: DKA causes metabolic acidosis (ketones). ABG: Low pH, low HCO3. Kussmaul respirations (compensatory hyperventilation) blow off CO2.

questions.text: A client with burns to both legs (36% TBSA) is at risk for:
questions.explanation: Major burns cause massive fluid shift (third-spacing), leading to hypovolemic shock. Aggressive fluid resuscitation needed. Monitor urine output, vitals.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hypernatremia?
questions.explanation: Hypernatremia (>145): Thirst, dry mucous membranes, restlessness, seizures. Causes: dehydration, diabetes insipidus. Give hypotonic fluids slowly.

questions.text: A client with hypomagnesemia will have:
questions.explanation: Hypomagnesemia (<1.5): Tremors, hyperreflexia, arrhythmias, positive Trousseau's/Chvostek's. Often occurs with hypocalcemia. Give magnesium sulfate IV.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates uncompensated respiratory alkalosis?
questions.explanation: Uncompensated respiratory alkalosis: pH >7.45, PaCO2 <35, HCO3 normal. Caused by hyperventilation (anxiety, pain, fever).

questions.text: A burn client develops stridor and hoarseness. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Stridor, hoarseness, singed nasal hairs indicate inhalation injury. Prepare for intubation. Give 100% oxygen. Monitor for airway obstruction.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hypercalcemia?
questions.explanation: Hypercalcemia (>10.5): 'Stones, bones, groans, moans' - kidney stones, bone pain, constipation, confusion. Causes: hyperparathyroidism, cancer. Give fluids, loop diuretics.

questions.text: A client with COPD has chronic respiratory acidosis. The kidneys compensate by:
questions.explanation: Kidneys compensate for respiratory acidosis by retaining bicarbonate (HCO3) to raise pH. Takes days. Respiratory compensation is faster (minutes-hours).

questions.text: A burn client should be placed in which position?
questions.explanation: Position of function prevents contractures: neck extended, shoulders abducted, hands in functional position. Use splints. Encourage ROM exercises.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hypermagnesemia?
questions.explanation: Hypermagnesemia (>2.5): Decreased reflexes, lethargy, respiratory depression, bradycardia, hypotension. Causes: renal failure, excessive antacids. Give calcium gluconate.

questions.text: A client is hyperventilating due to anxiety. Which acid-base imbalance will occur?
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation blows off CO2, causing respiratory alkalosis. Have client breathe into paper bag (rebreathe CO2) or use relaxation techniques.

questions.text: A burn client develops curling's ulcer. This is:
questions.explanation: Curling's ulcer: Stress ulcer in burn clients. Prevent with H2 blockers or PPIs. Monitor for GI bleeding (coffee-ground emesis, melena).

questions.text: Which electrolyte imbalance causes peaked T waves on ECG?
questions.explanation: Hyperkalemia causes peaked T waves, widened QRS, eventually sine wave pattern. Life-threatening arrhythmias. Give calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose.

questions.text: A client with vomiting has which acid-base imbalance?
questions.explanation: Vomiting causes loss of stomach acid (HCl), leading to metabolic alkalosis. ABG: High pH, high HCO3. Also causes hypokalemia, hypochloremia.

questions.text: A burn client needs escharotomy. This is done to:
options: Relieve compartment syndrome from circumferential burns
questions.explanation: Escharotomy: Surgical incision through eschar to relieve compartment syndrome from circumferential full-thickness burns. Restores circulation.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hypokalemia on ECG?
questions.explanation: Hypokalemia: Flat/inverted T waves, prominent U waves, ST depression, prolonged QT. Risk of arrhythmias. Give potassium cautiously.

questions.text: A client with diarrhea has which acid-base imbalance?
questions.explanation: Diarrhea causes loss of bicarbonate, leading to metabolic acidosis. ABG: Low pH, low HCO3. Also causes dehydration, hypokalemia.

questions.text: A burn client should have urine output of at least:
questions.explanation: Target urine output: 30-50 mL/hr (adults), 1 mL/kg/hr (children). Indicates adequate fluid resuscitation. Adjust IV fluids based on output.

questions.text: Which electrolyte imbalance causes prolonged QT interval?
questions.explanation: Hypocalcemia causes prolonged QT interval, risk of torsades de pointes. Hypercalcemia causes shortened QT.

questions.text: A client with renal failure has which acid-base imbalance?
questions.explanation: Renal failure causes metabolic acidosis (can't excrete H+ or reabsorb HCO3). ABG: Low pH, low HCO3. May need dialysis.

questions.text: A burn client develops sepsis. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Sepsis: Broad-spectrum antibiotics, aggressive fluid resuscitation, vasopressors if needed. Monitor vitals, labs. Leading cause of death in burn clients.

questions.text: Which is a sign of hypophosphatemia?
questions.explanation: Hypophosphatemia: Muscle weakness, confusion, respiratory failure, hemolytic anemia. Causes: refeeding syndrome, malnutrition, alcoholism.

questions.text: A client with NG suction has which acid-base imbalance?
questions.explanation: NG suction removes stomach acid, causing metabolic alkalosis. ABG: High pH, high HCO3. Also causes hypokalemia, hypochloremia.

questions.text: A burn client needs silver sulfadiazine (Silvadene). What should the nurse know?
questions.explanation: Silver sulfadiazine: Topical antimicrobial for partial-thickness burns. Avoid if sulfa allergy. Don't use on face. Apply 1-2x daily after cleaning.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates partially compensated respiratory acidosis?
questions.explanation: Partially compensated: pH abnormal, both PaCO2 and HCO3 abnormal. Respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation (kidneys retaining HCO3).

questions.text: A burn client develops contractures. Prevention includes:
questions.explanation: Prevent contractures: ROM exercises, splinting, position of function, early mobilization. Occupational/physical therapy essential.

questions.text: Which is a sign of fluid overload?
questions.explanation: Fluid overload: Crackles, JVD, peripheral edema, weight gain, hypertension. Give diuretics, restrict fluids/sodium.

questions.text: A client with hyperventilation has tingling in fingers. This is due to:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation causes respiratory alkalosis, leading to paresthesias (tingling). Have client breathe into paper bag or use relaxation techniques.

questions.text: A burn client needs autograft. This is:
questions.explanation: Autograft: Client's own skin (permanent). Allograft: Cadaver skin (temporary). Xenograft: Pig skin (temporary). Synthetic skin also available.

questions.text: Which electrolyte should be monitored closely with loop diuretics?
questions.explanation: Loop diuretics (furosemide) cause potassium loss. Monitor for hypokalemia. Also lose calcium, magnesium. Thiazides are potassium-wasting too.

questions.text: A client with burns needs high-protein diet. This is because:
options: Promotes wound healing and prevents catabolism
questions.explanation: Burns cause hypermetabolism and catabolism. Need high-protein, high-calorie diet for wound healing. May need tube feeding or TPN.

questions.text: Which ABG indicates uncompensated metabolic acidosis?
questions.explanation: Uncompensated metabolic acidosis: pH <7.35, HCO3 <22, PaCO2 normal. No respiratory compensation yet.

questions.text: A burn client develops ARDS. This is:
questions.explanation: ARDS: Acute respiratory distress syndrome. Causes: inhalation injury, sepsis, fluid overload. Requires mechanical ventilation, PEEP.

questions.text: Which is the most important intervention for electrolyte imbalances?
questions.explanation: Always identify and treat underlying cause of electrolyte imbalances (medications, disease, diet). Correct imbalances cautiously to avoid complications.

title: GU, Musculoskeletal & Integumentary
description: UTI, Kidney Disease, Fractures, Arthritis, Burns, Wounds

questions.text: A client with a UTI should:
