questions.explanation: UTI: Increase fluids (flushes bacteria), take full course of antibiotics, void frequently, wipe front to back. Cranberry juice may help prevent recurrence.

questions.text: What are the '5 P's' of neurovascular assessment?
questions.explanation: 5 P's assess compartment syndrome/neurovascular compromise: Pain, Pallor, Pulse (absent/diminished), Paresthesia (numbness/tingling), Paralysis. Report immediately.

questions.text: A client with a cast reports severe pain unrelieved by medication. This may indicate:
questions.explanation: Compartment syndrome: Severe pain unrelieved by meds, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis. Medical emergency. Cast must be split/removed immediately.

questions.text: A client with chronic kidney disease should restrict:
questions.explanation: CKD: Restrict protein, potassium, phosphorus, sodium, fluids. Monitor for hyperkalemia, fluid overload, anemia. May need dialysis.

questions.text: What is the Rule of Nines for burns?
options: Estimates percentage of body surface burned
questions.explanation: Rule of Nines estimates burn area: Head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%.

questions.text: A client with rheumatoid arthritis should:
questions.explanation: RA: Balance rest and exercise, use heat for stiffness, cold for inflammation. Medications: NSAIDs, DMARDs, biologics. Protect joints.

questions.text: A client on hemodialysis should avoid:
questions.explanation: Protect AV fistula: No BP, IV, or blood draws on fistula arm. Check for thrill/bruit. Monitor for infection, clotting.

questions.text: Which burn involves epidermis and dermis with blisters?
questions.explanation: Partial-thickness (2nd degree): Blisters, red/moist, painful. Superficial: Red, no blisters. Full-thickness: White/charred, painless (nerve damage).

questions.text: A client with a hip fracture is at risk for:
questions.explanation: Hip/long bone fractures risk fat embolism. Signs: Dyspnea, petechiae, confusion. Also risk DVT, infection, delayed healing.

questions.text: A client with acute kidney injury has potassium of 6.8. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Critical hyperkalemia (>6.5): Give calcium gluconate (protects heart), insulin/glucose, kayexalate, possible dialysis. Monitor ECG for arrhythmias.

questions.text: A client post-hip replacement should avoid:
options: Hip flexion >90°, crossing legs, internal rotation
questions.explanation: Post-hip replacement: Avoid hip flexion >90°, crossing legs, internal rotation (risk dislocation). Use abduction pillow, elevated toilet seat.

questions.text: Which is a sign of acute kidney injury?
questions.explanation: AKI: Oliguria (<400 mL/day), elevated BUN/creatinine, fluid overload, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis. Treat underlying cause.

questions.text: A client with osteoporosis should:
options: Increase calcium, vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise
questions.explanation: Osteoporosis: Calcium 1200-1500 mg/day, vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, bisphosphonates. Prevent falls. DEXA scan monitors bone density.

questions.text: What is the priority intervention for a burn victim?
questions.explanation: Burn priority: Airway (inhalation injury risk), breathing, circulation. Stop burning process, remove jewelry, cover with clean cloth, IV fluids for large burns.

questions.text: A client with gout should avoid:
questions.explanation: Gout: Avoid purines (organ meats, shellfish, alcohol), increase fluids, take medications (colchicine, allopurinol). Affects big toe typically.

questions.text: A client on peritoneal dialysis develops cloudy dialysate. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Cloudy dialysate indicates peritonitis (infection). Also: abdominal pain, fever. Send sample for culture, give antibiotics. Clear dialysate is normal.

questions.text: Which position is best for a client with a hip fracture before surgery?
questions.explanation: Hip fracture: Keep affected leg abducted (pillow between legs), neutral rotation, minimal movement. Assess neurovascular status. Prepare for surgery.

questions.text: A client with a full-thickness burn will have:
questions.explanation: Full-thickness (3rd degree): White/brown/charred, dry, painless (nerves destroyed). Requires skin grafting. Partial-thickness is painful.

questions.text: A client with urinary retention should:
questions.explanation: Urinary retention: Catheterization (straight or indwelling), treat underlying cause (BPH, medications, neurological). Monitor for bladder distention.

questions.text: Which is a sign of compartment syndrome?
questions.explanation: Compartment syndrome: Severe pain (especially with passive stretching), pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, tense compartment. Emergency fasciotomy needed.

questions.text: A client with kidney stones should:
questions.explanation: Kidney stones: Increase fluids (prevents formation), strain urine, pain management. Lithotripsy or surgery for large stones. Prevent recurrence based on stone type.

questions.text: A client with a fracture has a cast applied. What should the nurse teach?
options: Report numbness, tingling, or severe pain
questions.explanation: Cast care: Keep dry, elevate to reduce swelling, report 5 P's (neurovascular compromise), don't insert objects, check skin at edges.

questions.text: Which type of burn is caused by chemicals?
questions.explanation: Burn types: Thermal (heat), Chemical (acids/alkalis - irrigate with water), Electrical (entry/exit wounds, cardiac monitoring), Radiation.

questions.text: A client with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) will have:
questions.explanation: BPH: Urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia, weak stream, retention. Treat with alpha blockers, 5-alpha reductase inhibitors, or TURP surgery.

questions.text: A client with osteoarthritis has pain that:
questions.explanation: Osteoarthritis: Pain worsens with activity, improves with rest. Joint stiffness, Heberden's nodes. Treat with NSAIDs, exercise, weight loss, joint replacement.

questions.text: What is the priority for a client with a major burn?
questions.explanation: Major burn priorities: Airway, fluid resuscitation (Parkland formula), prevent infection, pain management, nutrition. Monitor for shock, infection, contractures.

questions.text: A client post-amputation reports pain in the missing limb. This is called:
questions.explanation: Phantom limb pain: Sensation of pain in amputated limb. Real phenomenon. Treat with medications, mirror therapy, desensitization.

questions.text: A client with acute glomerulonephritis will have:
questions.explanation: Glomerulonephritis: Tea/cola-colored urine (hematuria), hypertension, edema, proteinuria. Often follows strep infection. Treat underlying cause, monitor kidney function.

questions.text: Which is a sign of fat embolism?
questions.explanation: Fat embolism (from long bone fractures): Petechiae (chest/axilla), dyspnea, confusion, tachycardia. Monitor respiratory status, give oxygen.

questions.text: A client with a pressure injury Stage 3 has:
questions.explanation: Pressure injury stages: 1=intact/red, 2=partial thickness/blister, 3=full thickness/crater, 4=muscle/bone exposed, Unstageable=eschar covering.

questions.text: A client with urinary incontinence should:
questions.explanation: Incontinence: Kegel exercises (strengthen pelvic floor), scheduled voiding, adequate fluids, treat underlying cause. Avoid bladder irritants (caffeine, alcohol).

questions.text: A client with a sprain should use RICE. This stands for:
questions.explanation: RICE for sprains/strains: Rest, Ice (first 24-48 hours), Compression (elastic bandage), Elevation (above heart). Then heat and gentle exercise.

questions.text: A client with end-stage renal disease will need:
questions.explanation: ESRD: Dialysis (hemodialysis or peritoneal) or kidney transplant. Strict diet restrictions, fluid limits, medications, monitor for complications.

questions.text: Which is the most common site for pressure injuries?
questions.explanation: Common pressure injury sites: Sacrum, heels, ischial tuberosities, greater trochanter, occiput. Prevent with repositioning q2h, pressure-relief devices.

questions.text: A client with carpal tunnel syndrome will have:
options: Numbness/tingling in thumb, index, middle fingers
questions.explanation: Carpal tunnel: Numbness/tingling in thumb, index, middle fingers (median nerve compression). Positive Tinel's/Phalen's sign. Treat with splint, NSAIDs, or surgery.

title: Immunology, Hematology & GI
description: HIV/AIDS, Anemia, Transfusions, Ulcers, IBD, Liver Disease

questions.text: A client with iron-deficiency anemia should increase intake of:
options: Red meat and dark leafy greens
questions.explanation: Iron-rich foods: red meat, dark leafy greens, beans. Take with vitamin C (citrus) to enhance absorption. Avoid taking with calcium/dairy.

questions.text: A client is receiving a blood transfusion and develops fever, chills, and back pain. What should the nurse do first?
questions.explanation: Fever, chills, back pain indicate transfusion reaction. Stop transfusion immediately, keep IV open with NS, notify provider, monitor vitals, save blood bag.

questions.text: A client with peptic ulcer disease should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid NSAIDs (cause ulcers), alcohol, caffeine, spicy foods. Milk provides temporary relief but increases acid production later.

questions.text: Which lab value indicates pernicious anemia?
questions.explanation: Pernicious anemia: B12 deficiency due to lack of intrinsic factor. Causes neurological symptoms. Treat with B12 injections.

questions.text: A client with Crohn's disease will have:
questions.explanation: Crohn's: Skip lesions (patchy), anywhere in GI tract, fistulas/abscesses common. Ulcerative colitis: continuous inflammation, colon/rectum only.

questions.text: A client with HIV has a CD4 count of 180. This indicates:
questions.explanation: CD4 <200 = AIDS diagnosis. Start prophylaxis for opportunistic infections (PCP, toxoplasmosis). Continue antiretroviral therapy.

questions.text: Which finding indicates hemolytic transfusion reaction?
options: Fever, chills, dark urine, flank pain
questions.explanation: Hemolytic reaction (ABO incompatibility): Fever, chills, dark urine, flank/back pain, hypotension. Life-threatening. Stop transfusion immediately.

questions.text: A client with cirrhosis has ascites. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: Ascites: Restrict sodium, give diuretics (spironolactone, furosemide), monitor weight daily, measure abdominal girth. May need paracentesis.

questions.text: Which type of anemia is caused by chronic kidney disease?
questions.explanation: Kidney disease causes decreased erythropoietin (EPO) production, leading to anemia. Treat with EPO injections (epoetin alfa).

questions.text: A client with ulcerative colitis is at risk for:
questions.explanation: Long-term ulcerative colitis increases colon cancer risk. Regular colonoscopy screening needed. May require colectomy.

questions.text: Which precaution is needed for a client with neutropenia?
questions.explanation: Neutropenia (low WBC <1,000): Protective isolation, no fresh fruits/flowers, avoid crowds, monitor for infection. Neutropenic precautions.

questions.text: A client is receiving warfarin and has INR of 8. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: INR >4 = high bleeding risk. Hold warfarin, notify provider. May need vitamin K. Monitor for bleeding. Therapeutic INR: 2-3.

questions.text: A client with hepatitis A should:
questions.explanation: Hepatitis A: Acute, fecal-oral transmission, avoid alcohol during illness. Hepatitis B/C: Chronic, bloodborne, avoid alcohol permanently.

questions.text: Which is a sign of sickle cell crisis?
questions.explanation: Sickle cell crisis: Severe pain (vaso-occlusive), fever, swelling. Treat with hydration, oxygen, pain management. Avoid triggers (dehydration, cold, stress).

questions.text: A client with GI bleeding has black, tarry stools. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Melena (black, tarry stools) = upper GI bleeding. Bright red blood (hematochezia) = lower GI bleeding. Monitor H/H, vitals, prepare for endoscopy.

questions.text: A client with thrombocytopenia should avoid:
questions.explanation: Thrombocytopenia (low platelets): Bleeding precautions. Avoid IM injections, aspirin, NSAIDs, razors. Use soft toothbrush, electric razor.

questions.text: Which hepatitis is transmitted through contaminated food/water?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis A: Fecal-oral (food/water). Hepatitis B, C, D: Bloodborne (needles, sex, blood products). Hepatitis E: Fecal-oral (rare in US).

questions.text: A client with anaphylaxis should receive:
questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine IM (EpiPen) immediately. Also give oxygen, IV fluids, antihistamines, corticosteroids. Monitor airway.

questions.text: A client with hemophilia should avoid:
questions.explanation: Hemophilia: Clotting factor deficiency. Avoid contact sports, aspirin, IM injections. Give factor replacement for bleeding. Teach joint protection.

questions.text: Which is a sign of liver failure?
questions.explanation: Liver failure: Jaundice, confusion (hepatic encephalopathy), ascites, bleeding (low clotting factors), asterixis (flapping tremor).

questions.text: A client is receiving a blood transfusion. What is the maximum time for one unit?
questions.explanation: Transfuse blood over 2-4 hours maximum (bacterial growth risk after 4 hours). Monitor vitals before, 15 min after start, then per protocol.

questions.text: A client with diverticulitis should eat:
options: Low-fiber diet during acute phase, then high-fiber
questions.explanation: Acute diverticulitis: Low-fiber or clear liquids, antibiotics. After healing: High-fiber diet to prevent recurrence. Avoid nuts/seeds (controversial).

questions.text: Which medication treats pernicious anemia?
questions.explanation: Pernicious anemia requires B12 injections (oral B12 not absorbed without intrinsic factor). Monitor for neurological improvement.

questions.text: A client with esophageal varices should avoid:
questions.explanation: Esophageal varices (from cirrhosis): Avoid straining, alcohol, aspirin, NSAIDs. Risk of life-threatening bleeding. May need banding or sclerotherapy.

questions.text: Which is a sign of allergic transfusion reaction?
questions.explanation: Allergic reaction: Hives, itching, flushing. Stop transfusion, give antihistamine. Can usually resume slowly. Different from hemolytic reaction.

questions.text: A client with hepatic encephalopathy should:
questions.explanation: Hepatic encephalopathy: Restrict protein (ammonia buildup), give lactulose (reduces ammonia), monitor mental status. Asterixis is classic sign.

questions.text: Which type of anemia has sickle-shaped RBCs?
questions.explanation: Sickle cell: Genetic disorder, RBCs sickle when deoxygenated. Causes pain crises, organ damage. Treat with hydration, oxygen, pain management, hydroxyurea.

questions.text: A client with pancreatitis should be NPO because:
questions.explanation: Pancreatitis: NPO to rest pancreas (food stimulates enzyme secretion). Give IV fluids, pain management, NG tube if vomiting. Monitor for complications.

questions.text: Which precaution is needed for HIV-positive client?
questions.explanation: HIV: Standard precautions (not transmitted by casual contact). Bloodborne pathogen. Use gloves for blood/body fluids. Educate about transmission prevention.

questions.text: A client with DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation) will have:
questions.explanation: DIC: Widespread clotting uses up clotting factors, causing bleeding. Treat underlying cause, give blood products, monitor labs (PT, PTT, platelets, fibrinogen).

questions.text: A client with gastroesophageal reflux (GERD) should:
options: Elevate head of bed and avoid late meals
questions.explanation: GERD: Elevate HOB 30°, small frequent meals, avoid triggers (caffeine, alcohol, chocolate, spicy foods), don't eat 2-3 hours before bed.

questions.text: Which is a sign of iron-deficiency anemia?
questions.explanation: Iron-deficiency: Pallor, fatigue, glossitis (sore tongue), koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails), pica (craving ice/dirt). Most common anemia.

questions.text: A client with inflammatory bowel disease should avoid:
questions.explanation: IBD flare: Low-fiber, low-residue diet. Between flares: Balanced diet, avoid individual triggers. Monitor for complications (obstruction, perforation).

questions.text: Which lab value indicates hemolytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Hemolytic anemia: RBC destruction causes elevated bilirubin (jaundice), elevated LDH, decreased haptoglobin. Causes: autoimmune, transfusion reaction, sickle cell.

questions.text: A client with cholecystitis reports pain in which area?
questions.explanation: Cholecystitis (gallbladder inflammation): Right upper quadrant pain, especially after fatty meals. May radiate to right shoulder. Treat with NPO, antibiotics, possible cholecystectomy.

title: Multi-System & OB Emergencies
description: Sepsis, Shock, Preeclampsia, Eclampsia, Placental Emergencies

questions.text: A client has BP 88/50, HR 120, cool clammy skin after blood loss. This is:
questions.explanation: Hypovolemic shock: Fluid/blood loss causes decreased BP, tachycardia, cool/clammy skin. Give IV fluids, blood products, treat bleeding source.

questions.text: A pregnant client has BP 160/110, 3+ proteinuria, and headache. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Preeclampsia: HTN (>140/90) after 20 weeks, proteinuria, edema. Severe: BP >160/110, headache, visual changes, epigastric pain. Give magnesium sulfate, deliver baby.

questions.text: Which type of shock has warm, flushed skin initially?
questions.explanation: Septic shock (early/warm phase): Warm, flushed skin, bounding pulses, fever. Later (cold phase): Cool, clammy skin. Give antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors.

questions.text: A client with preeclampsia is receiving magnesium sulfate. Which finding indicates toxicity?
questions.explanation: Magnesium toxicity: Respiratory rate <12, absent DTRs, decreased urine output. Antidote: Calcium gluconate. Therapeutic level: 4-7 mEq/L.

questions.text: A client in septic shock needs which intervention first?
options: Blood cultures, then antibiotics within 1 hour
questions.explanation: Sepsis bundle: Blood cultures first, then broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, IV fluids, monitor lactate, vasopressors if needed.

questions.text: A pregnant client has painless bright red vaginal bleeding. This suggests:
questions.explanation: Placenta previa: Painless bright red bleeding (placenta covers cervix). No vaginal exams! Bed rest, C-section delivery. Abruptio: Painful dark bleeding.

questions.text: Which is a sign of cardiogenic shock?
options: Pulmonary edema and decreased cardiac output
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock: Heart can't pump (MI, CHF). Pulmonary edema, decreased CO, cool/clammy skin. Treat with inotropes, diuretics, possible mechanical support.

questions.text: A client with eclampsia is having a seizure. What is the priority?
options: Protect from injury, turn to side, give oxygen
questions.explanation: Eclampsia (seizure from preeclampsia): Protect from injury, turn to side, oxygen, magnesium sulfate IV. Deliver baby after stabilization.

questions.text: Which type of shock results from spinal cord injury?
questions.explanation: Neurogenic shock: Spinal cord injury causes loss of sympathetic tone. Hypotension, bradycardia, warm/dry skin. Treat with fluids, vasopressors, atropine.

questions.text: A pregnant client has severe abdominal pain, rigid abdomen, and dark vaginal bleeding. This suggests:
questions.explanation: Abruptio placentae: Placental separation causes severe pain, rigid/board-like abdomen, dark bleeding, fetal distress. Emergency C-section, monitor for DIC.

questions.text: What is the first sign of shock?
questions.explanation: Early shock: Tachycardia (compensatory), restlessness, cool extremities. Late shock: Hypotension, decreased LOC, oliguria. Treat early!

questions.text: A postpartum client has heavy bleeding and a boggy uterus. What should the nurse do first?
questions.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage: Massage fundus to stimulate contraction, empty bladder, give oxytocin, monitor vitals. Boggy uterus = uterine atony (most common cause).

questions.text: Which lab value indicates SIRS (Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome)?
questions.explanation: SIRS criteria (2+ needed): Temp >38°C or <36°C, HR >90, RR >20, WBC >12,000 or <4,000. With infection = sepsis.

questions.text: A client with preeclampsia should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: HELLP syndrome: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets. Severe complication of preeclampsia. Symptoms: RUQ pain, nausea, malaise. Deliver baby.

questions.text: Which position is best for a client in shock?
questions.explanation: Shock position: Supine with legs elevated 20-30° (modified Trendelenburg) improves venous return. Full Trendelenburg can impair breathing.

questions.text: A laboring client has the umbilical cord protruding from vagina. What is the priority?
options: Elevate hips, cover cord with sterile saline gauze, prepare for C-section
questions.explanation: Prolapsed cord: Elevate hips (knee-chest or Trendelenburg), cover cord with sterile saline gauze, don't push back, emergency C-section. Relieve pressure on cord.

questions.text: Which is a sign of anaphylactic shock?
questions.explanation: Anaphylactic shock: Urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, hypotension. Give epinephrine IM immediately, oxygen, IV fluids, antihistamines, corticosteroids.

questions.text: A client with severe preeclampsia should be in which environment?
questions.explanation: Severe preeclampsia: Quiet, darkened room to prevent seizures. Limit stimulation, monitor BP/reflexes, magnesium sulfate, prepare for delivery.

questions.text: What is the goal of shock treatment?
questions.explanation: Shock treatment goals: Restore tissue perfusion, maintain BP, adequate urine output (30-50 mL/hr), normal lactate, mental status. Treat underlying cause.

questions.text: A postpartum client has fever, foul-smelling lochia, and uterine tenderness. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Endometritis: Uterine infection. Fever, foul lochia, uterine tenderness. Give broad-spectrum antibiotics. Risk factors: C-section, prolonged rupture of membranes.

questions.text: Which medication is used to treat septic shock hypotension?
questions.explanation: Septic shock: Norepinephrine (first-line vasopressor) if hypotensive despite fluids. Also: antibiotics, IV fluids (30 mL/kg), monitor lactate.

questions.text: A client at 32 weeks gestation has contractions. Which medication stops labor?
questions.explanation: Tocolytics (terbutaline, nifedipine) stop preterm labor. Magnesium sulfate also used. Give corticosteroids (betamethasone) for fetal lung maturity.

questions.text: Which is a late sign of shock?
questions.explanation: Late shock signs: Hypotension, decreased LOC, oliguria, metabolic acidosis. Early signs: Tachycardia, restlessness, cool extremities. Intervene early!

questions.text: A laboring client has fetal head delivered but shoulders stuck. This is:
questions.explanation: Shoulder dystocia: Shoulders stuck after head delivery. McRoberts maneuver (flex hips), suprapubic pressure, call for help. Risk: large baby, maternal diabetes.

questions.text: Which organ fails first in MODS (Multiple Organ Dysfunction Syndrome)?
questions.explanation: MODS: Progressive organ failure from severe illness/sepsis. Lungs often fail first (ARDS), then kidneys, liver, heart. High mortality. Supportive care.

questions.text: A pregnant client has BP 170/115 and is seeing spots. What medication is given?
questions.explanation: Severe preeclampsia: Magnesium sulfate prevents seizures. Also: antihypertensives (hydralazine, labetalol), monitor reflexes/respirations, deliver baby.

questions.text: Which is a sign of distributive shock?
questions.explanation: Distributive shock (septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic): Vasodilation, decreased SVR, relative hypovolemia. Treat with fluids, vasopressors, address cause.

questions.text: A postpartum client has saturated 2 pads in 30 minutes. This is:
questions.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage: >500 mL vaginal, >1000 mL C-section, or saturating pad in <1 hour. Massage fundus, oxytocin, monitor vitals, possible surgery.

questions.text: Which lab value indicates inadequate tissue perfusion in shock?
questions.explanation: Elevated lactate (>2 mmol/L) indicates anaerobic metabolism from poor tissue perfusion. Monitor lactate clearance to assess shock treatment effectiveness.

questions.text: A client with preeclampsia has hyperreflexia and clonus. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Hyperreflexia and clonus indicate CNS irritability and increased seizure risk. Give magnesium sulfate, monitor closely, prepare for possible eclampsia.

questions.text: Which is the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage?
questions.explanation: Uterine atony (70%): Boggy uterus, heavy bleeding. Massage fundus, empty bladder, oxytocin, methergine. Other causes: lacerations, retained placenta, coagulopathy.

questions.text: A client in shock has urine output 15 mL/hr. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Oliguria (<30 mL/hr) indicates inadequate renal perfusion. Goal: 30-50 mL/hr. Monitor hourly urine output, give fluids, vasopressors if needed.

questions.text: A pregnant client has gestational diabetes. The baby is at risk for:
questions.explanation: Maternal diabetes → fetal hyperinsulinemia. After birth, baby loses maternal glucose → hypoglycemia. Monitor glucose, feed early, possible IV dextrose. Also risk: macrosomia, shoulder dystocia.

questions.text: Which is a contraindication for magnesium sulfate?
questions.explanation: Magnesium contraindications: Absent DTRs, respiratory rate <12, renal failure, myasthenia gravis. Monitor reflexes, respirations, urine output. Have calcium gluconate ready.

questions.text: A client in shock is receiving IV fluids. Which finding indicates fluid overload?
questions.explanation: Fluid overload: Crackles, JVD, dyspnea, edema. Monitor lung sounds, daily weights, I&O. May need diuretics. Balance fluid resuscitation with risk of overload.

title: Final Prep Review
description: Test Anxiety & Final Concept Checks

questions.text: Study strategy question...
questions.explanation: Explanation.

title: Psychosocial Integrity (Unassigned)
description: Therapeutic Communication, Abuse Recognition, Grief, Mental Health

questions.text: Which therapeutic communication technique is best for a grieving client?
questions.explanation: Silence and active listening allow the client to express feelings without judgment or pressure, facilitating the grief process.

questions.text: According to Kübler-Ross, what is the first stage of grief?
questions.explanation: The five stages of grief are: Denial, Anger, Bargaining, Depression, and Acceptance (DABDA).

questions.text: A nurse suspects child abuse. What is the priority action?
options: Report to appropriate authorities per facility protocol
Document suspicions in the chart only
questions.explanation: Nurses are mandated reporters. Suspected abuse must be reported immediately to child protective services or appropriate authorities.

questions.text: Which statement is an example of therapeutic communication?
options: Tell me more about how you're feeling.
questions.explanation: Open-ended questions like 'Tell me more...' encourage the client to express feelings and thoughts. 'Why' questions can be defensive.

questions.text: A client says 'I'm going to kill myself.' What is the nurse's priority response?
options: Ask if the client has a specific plan
Tell the client not to talk that way
Reassure the client things will get better
questions.explanation: Assess suicide risk by asking about plan, means, and intent. Never leave a suicidal client alone; stay and call for help.

questions.text: Which finding is most indicative of elder abuse?
options: Multiple bruises in various stages of healing
questions.explanation: Bruises in various stages of healing suggest repeated trauma and are a red flag for physical abuse.

questions.text: A client in the anger stage of grief yells at the nurse. What is the best response?
options: Tell the client to calm down
Remain calm and allow the client to express feelings
questions.explanation: Anger is a normal grief stage. Remain calm, set boundaries if needed, and allow safe expression of feelings.

questions.text: Which communication technique should be AVOIDED?
questions.explanation: False reassurance (e.g., 'Everything will be fine') dismisses client concerns and blocks therapeutic communication.

questions.text: A client with depression says 'I'm worthless.' What is the therapeutic response?
options: Why do you feel that way?
You sound like you're feeling pretty low about yourself.
questions.explanation: Reflection acknowledges feelings without judgment. Avoid 'why' questions and false reassurance.

questions.text: Which behavior suggests a child is being sexually abused?
questions.explanation: Age-inappropriate sexual knowledge or behavior is a strong indicator of sexual abuse and requires immediate reporting.

questions.text: A client in the bargaining stage of grief might say:
options: This can't be happening to me.
If I pray more, maybe I'll get better.
I just feel so sad all the time.
questions.explanation: Bargaining involves attempting to negotiate (often with a higher power) to change the outcome.

questions.text: Which is a priority assessment for a client with suicidal ideation?
questions.explanation: Assessing access to lethal means (guns, pills, etc.) is critical for safety. Remove access to means if possible.

questions.text: A nurse observes a parent yelling and threatening a child. What should the nurse do?
options: Ignore it as a private family matter
Intervene calmly and assess the situation
questions.explanation: Intervene to ensure child safety, assess the situation, and report per protocol. Emotional abuse is reportable.

questions.text: Which statement demonstrates empathy?
options: I know exactly how you feel.
This must be very difficult for you.
questions.explanation: Empathy acknowledges the client's feelings without claiming to know exactly how they feel or minimizing their experience.

questions.text: A client with anxiety is hyperventilating. What is the priority intervention?
options: Have client breathe into a paper bag
questions.explanation: Coach slow, deep breathing to reduce hyperventilation. Paper bags are no longer recommended due to hypoxia risk.

questions.text: Which is a sign of domestic violence?
options: Partner answers all questions for the client
questions.explanation: Controlling behavior (partner answering for client, not allowing privacy) is a red flag for domestic violence.

questions.text: In the acceptance stage of grief, a client might say:
options: I can't believe this is real.
Why is this happening to me?
I'm ready to face what's ahead.
If only I had done things differently.
questions.explanation: Acceptance involves coming to terms with reality and finding peace with the situation.

questions.text: A client with schizophrenia says 'The TV is sending me messages.' What is the therapeutic response?
options: I don't hear any messages, but I understand this is real to you.
questions.explanation: Acknowledge the client's reality without reinforcing the delusion. Don't argue or agree with delusions.

questions.text: Which is a risk factor for suicide?
questions.explanation: Previous suicide attempt is the strongest predictor of future attempts. Other risks: depression, substance abuse, isolation, access to means.

questions.text: A nurse suspects a colleague is impaired by substances. What should the nurse do?
options: Report to the nurse manager per facility policy
questions.explanation: Patient safety is priority. Report suspected impairment to the nurse manager or appropriate authority per facility protocol.

questions.text: Which communication technique involves restating the client's message?
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing restates the client's message in the nurse's own words to ensure understanding.

questions.text: A client with PTSD is experiencing a flashback. What is the priority intervention?
options: Orient the client to the present
questions.explanation: During flashbacks, orient the client to the present using grounding techniques (e.g., 'You are safe. You are in the hospital.').

questions.text: Which is a sign of neglect in an elderly client?
questions.explanation: Poor hygiene, malnutrition, untreated medical conditions, and unsafe living conditions indicate neglect.

questions.text: A client says 'I feel like I'm going crazy.' What is the best response?
options: Tell me what you mean by that.
questions.explanation: Clarification helps understand the client's meaning. Avoid false reassurance or dismissing feelings.

questions.text: Which is an appropriate intervention for a client with depression?
options: Allow the client to sleep all day
Tell the client to cheer up
questions.explanation: Encourage activity and socialization. Depression worsens with isolation and inactivity.

questions.text: A client with mania is pacing and talking rapidly. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: Reduce stimulation to decrease agitation. Use short, simple statements. Ensure safety.

questions.text: Which behavior suggests a therapeutic nurse-client relationship?
options: Visiting the client at home after discharge
questions.explanation: Professional boundaries protect both nurse and client. Avoid dual relationships and maintain therapeutic focus.

questions.text: A client with anorexia nervosa is exercising excessively. What should the nurse do?
options: Allow exercise as a coping mechanism
Set limits on exercise per treatment plan
Encourage more exercise to build appetite
questions.explanation: Excessive exercise is a compensatory behavior in eating disorders. Set limits and monitor per treatment plan.

questions.text: Which is a therapeutic response to a client who is crying?
options: Don't cry, things will get better.
questions.explanation: Therapeutic presence (sitting quietly, offering tissues) shows support without interrupting the emotional release.

questions.text: A client with alcohol withdrawal is experiencing hallucinations. What is the priority?
options: Ensure safety and administer prescribed medications
questions.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal can cause delirium tremens with hallucinations. Ensure safety, administer benzodiazepines as ordered, monitor vital signs.

questions.text: Which statement indicates a client is in the depression stage of grief?
options: I can't believe this is happening.
I just want to be left alone. Nothing matters anymore.
If I change my lifestyle, maybe I'll recover.
questions.explanation: Depression stage involves sadness, withdrawal, and feelings of hopelessness about the loss.

questions.text: A client with OCD is washing hands repeatedly. What is the appropriate intervention?
options: Allow the behavior initially, then gradually set limits
questions.explanation: Don't abruptly stop compulsions (increases anxiety). Gradually set limits while teaching alternative coping strategies.

questions.text: Which is a priority when caring for a client in restraints?
options: Leave restraints on continuously for 8 hours
Document only at the end of shift
questions.explanation: Monitor circulation, skin integrity, and vital signs every 15-30 minutes. Release restraints every 2 hours for ROM and toileting.

questions.text: A client says 'I hear voices telling me to hurt myself.' What is the priority action?
options: Ask what the voices are saying
Ensure immediate safety and stay with the client
Tell the client the voices aren't real
questions.explanation: Command hallucinations to harm self or others require immediate safety measures. Stay with client, remove dangerous objects, notify provider.

questions.text: Which intervention promotes a therapeutic environment for mental health clients?
options: Allowing clients to do whatever they want
questions.explanation: Structure, consistency, and clear expectations provide safety and reduce anxiety in therapeutic environments.

title: Maternity & OB Nursing
description: Maternity: Antepartum, Intrapartum, Postpartum, Newborn Care

questions.text: A pregnant client at 10 weeks gestation reports nausea and vomiting. Which recommendation is most appropriate?
options: Eat three large meals per day
Eat small, frequent meals and avoid spicy foods
Drink large amounts of fluids with meals
questions.explanation: Small, frequent meals help manage morning sickness. Avoiding spicy, fatty foods and separating liquids from solids can reduce nausea.

questions.text: A nurse is teaching a prenatal class about fetal development. At what gestational age can the fetal heartbeat typically be heard with a Doppler?
questions.explanation: Fetal heart tones can typically be detected with a Doppler device at 10-12 weeks gestation. Earlier detection may be possible with transvaginal ultrasound.

questions.text: A client at 28 weeks gestation has a positive glucose challenge test. What is the next step?
questions.explanation: A positive glucose challenge test requires confirmation with a 3-hour glucose tolerance test before diagnosing gestational diabetes.

questions.text: Which finding in a pregnant client at 32 weeks gestation requires immediate intervention?
options: Mild ankle edema at end of day
questions.explanation: Severe headache with visual changes indicates possible preeclampsia, requiring immediate assessment of blood pressure and other symptoms.

questions.text: A nurse is teaching about warning signs during pregnancy. Which symptom should be reported immediately?
options: Sudden gush of fluid from vagina
questions.explanation: Sudden gush of fluid suggests rupture of membranes, requiring immediate evaluation to assess for infection risk and fetal well-being.

questions.text: What is the recommended weight gain during pregnancy for a woman with normal pre-pregnancy BMI?
questions.explanation: For women with normal BMI (18.5-24.9), the recommended weight gain is 25-35 pounds during pregnancy.

questions.text: A laboring client's membranes rupture spontaneously. What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.explanation: After rupture of membranes, immediately assess fetal heart rate to detect cord prolapse or compression, which can cause fetal distress.

questions.text: A client in active labor has contractions every 3 minutes lasting 60 seconds. The cervix is dilated 6 cm. What stage of labor is this?
questions.explanation: Active phase of first stage labor is characterized by cervical dilation of 4-7 cm with regular, strong contractions.

questions.text: Which fetal heart rate pattern requires immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency and fetal hypoxia, requiring immediate intervention (position change, oxygen, IV fluids).

questions.text: A client receives an epidural for labor pain. What is the priority nursing assessment?
questions.explanation: Hypotension is a common side effect of epidural anesthesia due to vasodilation. Monitor blood pressure frequently and position client on left side.

questions.text: Which finding indicates the transition phase of labor?
options: Cervix dilated 8-10 cm with intense contractions
questions.explanation: Transition phase occurs at 8-10 cm dilation with very strong, frequent contractions. Client may feel overwhelmed and irritable.

questions.text: A client is pushing during second stage of labor. Which position is most effective?
questions.explanation: Upright positions (squatting, semi-sitting) use gravity to assist descent and allow pelvic diameter to increase, facilitating delivery.

questions.text: When should the nurse expect delivery of the placenta after birth?
questions.explanation: The third stage of labor (placental delivery) typically occurs within 5-30 minutes after birth. Prolonged third stage increases hemorrhage risk.

questions.text: A postpartum client has a boggy uterus. What is the first nursing action?
questions.explanation: A boggy (soft, uncontracted) uterus indicates uterine atony. First action is to massage the fundus to stimulate contraction and prevent hemorrhage.

questions.text: What is the expected location of the fundus immediately after delivery?
questions.explanation: Immediately postpartum, the fundus should be firm and at or slightly below the umbilicus. It descends approximately 1 cm per day.

questions.text: A breastfeeding mother asks about afterpains. What should the nurse explain?
options: They are caused by oxytocin release during breastfeeding
They should not occur after the first birth
questions.explanation: Afterpains are uterine contractions caused by oxytocin release during breastfeeding. They are more common in multiparas and help prevent hemorrhage.

questions.text: Which lochia characteristic on postpartum day 1 is normal?
questions.explanation: Lochia rubra (red, bloody discharge) with small clots is normal for days 1-3 postpartum. Large clots or excessive bleeding require assessment.

questions.text: A postpartum client reports severe perineal pain. What should the nurse assess first?
questions.explanation: Severe perineal pain may indicate hematoma formation. Assess for swelling, discoloration, and asymmetry of the perineum.

questions.text: What is the priority teaching for a client with postpartum preeclampsia?
options: Report headache or visual changes immediately
questions.explanation: Postpartum preeclampsia can progress to eclampsia (seizures). Teach client to report warning signs: severe headache, visual changes, epigastric pain.

questions.text: When should a postpartum client resume sexual activity?
options: After lochia stops and perineum heals (4-6 weeks)
questions.explanation: Sexual activity should be delayed until lochia has stopped and perineal healing is complete, typically 4-6 weeks postpartum.

questions.text: A newborn's Apgar score at 1 minute is 8. What does this indicate?
options: Good condition with minimal intervention needed
questions.explanation: Apgar scores: 7-10 = good condition, 4-6 = moderate distress, 0-3 = severe distress. Score of 8 indicates good adaptation to extrauterine life.

questions.text: What is the normal respiratory rate for a newborn?
questions.explanation: Normal newborn respiratory rate is 30-60 breaths per minute. Rates above 60 may indicate respiratory distress.

questions.text: A newborn has a heart rate of 180 bpm while crying. What should the nurse do?
questions.explanation: Normal newborn heart rate is 120-160 bpm at rest, but can increase to 180 bpm when crying. Reassess when calm to determine true baseline.

questions.text: What is the purpose of administering vitamin K to a newborn?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K is given IM to prevent hemorrhagic disease of the newborn. Newborns have sterile intestines and cannot produce vitamin K initially.

questions.text: Erythromycin eye ointment is administered to newborns to prevent which infection?
questions.explanation: Erythromycin eye ointment prevents ophthalmia neonatorum (eye infection) caused by gonorrhea or chlamydia transmitted during vaginal delivery.

questions.text: A newborn's axillary temperature is 97.2°F (36.2°C). What is the priority action?
options: Place under radiant warmer and reassess
questions.explanation: Normal newborn temperature is 97.7-99.5°F (36.5-37.5°C). Temperature of 97.2°F indicates hypothermia requiring warming and reassessment.

questions.text: What is the best way to assess for adequate breastfeeding in a newborn?
options: 6-8 wet diapers and 3-4 stools per day by day 4
questions.explanation: Adequate hydration and nutrition are indicated by 6-8 wet diapers and 3-4 stools daily by day 4. Newborns should feed 8-12 times per 24 hours.

questions.text: A newborn develops jaundice on day 2 of life. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Physiologic jaundice appears after 24 hours due to normal RBC breakdown and immature liver. Jaundice before 24 hours is pathologic and requires investigation.

questions.text: What is the correct technique for umbilical cord care?
options: Keep clean and dry, fold diaper below cord
questions.explanation: Keep cord clean and dry. Fold diaper below cord to prevent urine contamination. Cord typically falls off in 10-14 days.

questions.text: A mother asks when to call the provider about her newborn. Which symptom requires immediate attention?
questions.explanation: Fever (≥100.4°F/38°C) in a newborn may indicate serious infection and requires immediate medical evaluation.

questions.text: What is the recommended sleep position for newborns to reduce SIDS risk?
questions.explanation: Back to sleep (supine position) significantly reduces SIDS risk. Avoid soft bedding, pillows, and bed-sharing.

questions.text: A breastfeeding mother reports sore, cracked nipples. What is the best advice?
options: Limit feeding to 5 minutes per breast
questions.explanation: Sore nipples usually result from poor latch. Assess positioning and ensure baby takes entire areola into mouth, not just the nipple.

questions.text: When should a newborn have the first bowel movement (meconium)?
questions.explanation: Meconium (first stool) should be passed within 24-48 hours. Failure to pass meconium may indicate intestinal obstruction or other problems.

questions.text: What is the normal newborn blood glucose level?
questions.explanation: Normal newborn glucose is 40-60 mg/dL in first hours, then 60-100 mg/dL. Levels <40 mg/dL indicate hypoglycemia requiring intervention.

questions.text: A mother asks about circumcision care. What should the nurse teach?
options: Apply petroleum jelly with each diaper change
questions.explanation: Apply petroleum jelly to prevent diaper from sticking to healing site. Yellow exudate is normal granulation tissue. Avoid tub baths until healed.

title: Basic Care Review (Unassigned)
description: Wound Care, Pressure Injuries, Mobility, Elimination, Comfort

questions.text: To prevent pressure ulcers, the nurse should turn the client every:
questions.explanation: Turning every 2 hours is the standard of care to relieve pressure and preserve skin integrity.

questions.text: A pressure injury with full-thickness skin loss and visible subcutaneous fat is classified as:
questions.explanation: Stage 3 involves full-thickness skin loss with visible fat. Muscle, tendon, and bone are NOT visible (that's Stage 4).

questions.text: Which nutrient is most important for wound healing?
questions.explanation: Protein is essential for tissue repair and wound healing. Vitamin C and zinc also support healing.

questions.text: A client has a Braden Scale score of 12. What does this indicate?
questions.explanation: Braden Scale: ≤12 = High risk, 13-14 = Moderate risk, 15-18 = Low risk, 19-23 = No risk. Lower scores = higher risk.

questions.text: When assisting a client to ambulate, where should the nurse stand?
questions.explanation: Stand on the weaker side to provide support and prevent falls. Use a gait belt for safety.

questions.text: A Stage 2 pressure injury is characterized by:
options: Partial-thickness skin loss with exposed dermis
Full-thickness skin loss with visible fat
Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone
questions.explanation: Stage 2 = Partial-thickness loss with exposed dermis, may appear as blister or shallow ulcer. Dermis is pink/red.

questions.text: Which intervention promotes bowel elimination in a bedridden client?
questions.explanation: Increase fiber, fluids, and activity (as tolerated) to promote bowel elimination. Avoid routine laxative use.

questions.text: What is the priority when caring for a client with a urinary catheter?
options: Empty the drainage bag when full
Keep the drainage bag below bladder level
Disconnect and reconnect tubing as needed
questions.explanation: Keep drainage bag below bladder level to prevent backflow and infection. Never disconnect tubing; maintain closed system.

questions.text: A client reports pain of 8/10. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: Severe pain (8/10) requires immediate intervention. Administer medication, then reassess effectiveness in 30-60 minutes.

questions.text: Which finding indicates a Stage 1 pressure injury?
questions.explanation: Stage 1 = Intact skin with non-blanchable redness (doesn't turn white when pressed). Skin may be warm and firm.

questions.text: When transferring a client from bed to wheelchair, what should the nurse do first?
options: Raise the bed to highest position
questions.explanation: Lock wheelchair brakes first for safety. Then position wheelchair at 45° angle to bed on client's stronger side.

questions.text: A wound with red, moist tissue and granulation is in which phase of healing?
questions.explanation: Proliferative phase shows granulation tissue (red, moist, bumpy). This is healthy healing tissue.

questions.text: Which assistive device provides the most support for ambulation?
questions.explanation: Walker provides maximum stability and support. Cane provides least support; crutches require upper body strength.

questions.text: What is the correct technique for measuring a wound?
options: Measure length x width x depth
questions.explanation: Measure length (head to toe), width (side to side), and depth (deepest point) in centimeters. Document consistently.

questions.text: A client with constipation should avoid which food?
questions.explanation: Cheese and other dairy products can worsen constipation. Encourage high-fiber foods, fluids, and activity.

questions.text: Which intervention reduces the risk of catheter-associated UTI?
questions.explanation: Perineal care with soap and water reduces infection risk. Maintain closed system; don't irrigate routinely or disconnect tubing.

questions.text: A client uses a cane. Which instruction is correct?
options: Move cane and stronger leg together
questions.explanation: Hold cane on stronger side. Move cane and weaker leg together, then stronger leg. Cane should be 6 inches to side.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a pressure-relieving mattress?
options: Distribute pressure evenly to prevent skin breakdown
questions.explanation: Pressure-relieving devices (foam, air, gel mattresses) distribute pressure to prevent pressure injuries in high-risk clients.

questions.text: Which complementary therapy is appropriate for pain management?
questions.explanation: Complementary therapies (guided imagery, music, massage, heat/cold) can enhance pain relief when used with medications.

questions.text: A client has a Stage 4 pressure injury. What is visible in the wound?
questions.explanation: Stage 4 = Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed muscle, tendon, or bone. High risk for osteomyelitis.

questions.text: When should postmortem care be performed?
questions.explanation: Perform postmortem care promptly after death is pronounced. Allow family time with body first if desired, respecting cultural practices.

questions.text: A client is on bedrest. Which intervention prevents complications?
options: Keep client flat at all times
questions.explanation: ROM exercises prevent contractures and maintain joint mobility. Also turn q2h, encourage deep breathing, and prevent DVT.

questions.text: Which wound requires a sterile dressing change?
questions.explanation: Surgical incisions require sterile technique. Chronic wounds (pressure injuries) use clean technique after initial healing.

questions.text: A client reports bladder fullness but cannot void. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Assess for urinary retention by palpating bladder. May need catheterization if unable to void and bladder is distended.

questions.text: What is the correct position for a client receiving an enema?
questions.explanation: Left lateral (Sims') position allows enema solution to flow along natural curve of sigmoid colon and rectum.

title: Cardiovascular & Diagnostics
description: COPD, Asthma, Pneumonia, Delirium, Stroke, Seizures, Diabetes, DKA

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum potassium?
questions.explanation: Normal potassium: 3.5-5.0 mEq/L. <3.5 = hypokalemia, >5.0 = hyperkalemia. Critical values require immediate intervention.

questions.text: A client with chest pain has elevated troponin levels. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Elevated troponin (cardiac enzyme) indicates myocardial damage/MI. Troponin is more specific than CK-MB for cardiac injury.

questions.text: What is the normal range for hemoglobin in adult males?
questions.explanation: Normal Hgb: Males 14-18 g/dL, Females 12-16 g/dL. Low Hgb indicates anemia; high Hgb may indicate polycythemia or dehydration.

questions.text: A client is experiencing an acute MI. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: MONA protocol for MI: Morphine (pain), Oxygen (if SpO2 <90%), Nitroglycerin (chest pain), Aspirin (antiplatelet). Also obtain 12-lead ECG immediately.

questions.text: What is the therapeutic INR range for a client on warfarin?
questions.explanation: Therapeutic INR: 2-3 for most indications (may be 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves). <2 = subtherapeutic, >4 = bleeding risk.

questions.text: A client with heart failure has crackles in lungs and peripheral edema. What medication is priority?
questions.explanation: Diuretics (furosemide) reduce fluid overload in CHF. Also use ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, and monitor daily weights.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum sodium?
questions.explanation: Normal sodium: 135-145 mEq/L. <135 = hyponatremia, >145 = hypernatremia. Affects neurological and cardiac function.

questions.text: A client post-cardiac catheterization should be monitored for:
questions.explanation: Post-cath: Monitor insertion site for bleeding/hematoma, check distal pulses, keep leg straight 4-6 hours, watch for contrast reaction and kidney function.

questions.text: What is the normal range for platelet count?
questions.explanation: Normal platelets: 150,000-400,000/mm³. <50,000 = bleeding risk, <20,000 = spontaneous bleeding risk. Implement bleeding precautions.

questions.text: A client with hypertension should limit sodium intake to:
questions.explanation: Recommended sodium: <2,300 mg/day for general population, <1,500 mg/day for hypertension. DASH diet emphasizes fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy.

questions.text: What is a normal fasting blood glucose level?
questions.explanation: Normal fasting glucose: 70-100 mg/dL. 100-125 = prediabetes, ≥126 = diabetes. Random glucose ≥200 with symptoms also indicates diabetes.

questions.text: A client is having a stroke. What assessment tool should the nurse use?
questions.explanation: FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911. Quick stroke screening tool. Also use NIH Stroke Scale for detailed assessment.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum creatinine?
questions.explanation: Normal creatinine: 0.6-1.2 mg/dL. Elevated creatinine indicates kidney dysfunction. Also monitor BUN (normal 8-20 mg/dL).

questions.text: A client with atrial fibrillation is at risk for:
questions.explanation: A-fib causes blood pooling in atria, forming clots that can embolize to brain (stroke). Anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) prevents stroke.

questions.text: What is the normal range for WBC count?
questions.explanation: Normal WBC: 4,500-11,000/mm³. <4,500 = leukopenia (infection risk), >11,000 = leukocytosis (infection, inflammation, leukemia).

questions.text: A client post-MI should avoid which activity?
questions.explanation: Avoid Valsalva maneuver (bearing down, straining) - increases cardiac workload. Prevent constipation with stool softeners. Gradual activity increase.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum calcium?
questions.explanation: Normal calcium: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL. Hypocalcemia causes tetany, seizures. Hypercalcemia causes kidney stones, confusion, cardiac arrhythmias.

questions.text: A client with left-sided heart failure will have:
questions.explanation: Left-sided HF: Pulmonary congestion (crackles, dyspnea, orthopnea). Right-sided HF: Peripheral edema, JVD, hepatomegaly, ascites.

questions.text: What is a critical low hemoglobin value requiring immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Hgb <7 g/dL is critical - may need transfusion. Symptoms: fatigue, pallor, tachycardia, dyspnea. Notify provider immediately.

questions.text: A client is prescribed nitroglycerin for angina. What should the nurse teach?
options: Take up to 3 doses, 5 minutes apart
questions.explanation: Sublingual NTG: Take 1 dose, repeat q5min x3 if needed. Call 911 if pain persists after 3 doses. Store in dark bottle, causes headache (normal).

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum magnesium?
questions.explanation: Normal magnesium: 1.5-2.5 mEq/L. Hypomagnesemia causes tremors, arrhythmias. Hypermagnesemia causes decreased reflexes, respiratory depression.

questions.text: A client with peripheral arterial disease should:
questions.explanation: PAD: Keep legs dependent (down) to improve arterial flow. Avoid crossing legs, tight clothing. Elevate legs for venous disease, not arterial.

questions.text: What is the normal range for HbA1c?
questions.explanation: Normal HbA1c: <5.7%. 5.7-6.4% = prediabetes, ≥6.5% = diabetes. HbA1c reflects average glucose over 2-3 months.

questions.text: A client is receiving thrombolytic therapy for stroke. What is the priority assessment?
questions.explanation: Monitor BP closely with thrombolytics - keep systolic <180 mmHg to prevent hemorrhagic stroke. Also monitor for bleeding (major complication).

questions.text: What is the normal range for BUN (blood urea nitrogen)?
questions.explanation: Normal BUN: 8-20 mg/dL. Elevated BUN indicates kidney dysfunction or dehydration. BUN:Creatinine ratio helps differentiate causes.

questions.text: A client with deep vein thrombosis should:
options: Keep leg elevated and immobile initially
questions.explanation: DVT: Elevate leg, bed rest initially, anticoagulation. Never massage (can dislodge clot causing PE). Gradual ambulation after anticoagulation started.

questions.text: What is a normal PT (prothrombin time)?
questions.explanation: Normal PT: 11-13 seconds. PT/INR monitors warfarin. aPTT (25-35 sec) monitors heparin. Don't confuse the two.

questions.text: A client with heart failure should be taught to:
questions.explanation: Daily weights detect fluid retention (report gain of 2-3 lbs in 1 day or 5 lbs in 1 week). Restrict sodium and fluids as ordered.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum albumin?
questions.explanation: Normal albumin: 3.5-5.0 g/dL. Low albumin indicates malnutrition, liver disease, or kidney disease. Affects wound healing and fluid balance.

questions.text: A client post-pacemaker insertion should avoid:
questions.explanation: Avoid MRI (can damage pacemaker). Modern pacemakers are safe with microwaves, cell phones. Avoid raising arm above shoulder on pacemaker side for 4-6 weeks.

questions.text: What is the normal range for aPTT (activated partial thromboplastin time)?
questions.explanation: Normal aPTT: 25-35 seconds. Therapeutic on heparin: 60-80 seconds (1.5-2x normal). aPTT monitors heparin, not warfarin.

questions.text: A client with angina should be taught to:
options: Stop activity and rest if pain occurs
questions.explanation: Angina: Stop activity, rest, take nitroglycerin. If pain persists after 3 NTG doses (15 min), call 911 - may be MI.

questions.text: What is the normal range for total cholesterol?
questions.explanation: Desirable total cholesterol: <200 mg/dL. 200-239 = borderline high, ≥240 = high. Also monitor LDL (<100), HDL (>40 men, >50 women), triglycerides (<150).

questions.text: A client with hypertensive crisis (BP 220/120) should receive:
questions.explanation: Hypertensive crisis requires IV antihypertensive (nitroprusside, labetalol) for rapid BP reduction. Monitor for end-organ damage (stroke, MI, kidney failure).

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum glucose (random)?
questions.explanation: Normal random glucose: <140 mg/dL. ≥200 with symptoms indicates diabetes. Fasting glucose is more accurate for diagnosis.

questions.text: A client with venous insufficiency should:
questions.explanation: Venous insufficiency: Elevate legs above heart to improve venous return. Wear compression stockings. Opposite of arterial disease.

questions.text: What is a critical high potassium level?
questions.explanation: Critical hyperkalemia: >6.5 mEq/L. Causes cardiac arrhythmias, can be fatal. ECG changes: peaked T waves, widened QRS. Give calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, kayexalate.

questions.text: A client is having a cardiac catheterization. What allergy is most important to assess?
questions.explanation: Assess shellfish/iodine allergy - contrast dye contains iodine. Also assess kidney function (contrast is nephrotoxic). Hydrate before and after procedure.

questions.text: What is the normal range for serum chloride?
questions.explanation: Normal chloride: 95-105 mEq/L. Usually follows sodium. Hypochloremia occurs with vomiting; hyperchloremia with dehydration.

questions.text: A client with cardiogenic shock will have:
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock: Decreased cardiac output, hypotension, pulmonary edema. Caused by MI, severe HF. Treat with inotropes, diuretics, possible mechanical support.

questions.text: What is the normal range for RBC count in adult males?
questions.explanation: Normal RBC: Males 4.5-5.5 million/mm³, Females 4.0-5.0 million/mm³. Low RBC indicates anemia; high RBC may indicate polycythemia.

questions.text: A client with stable angina experiences chest pain:
questions.explanation: Stable angina: Predictable chest pain with exertion, relieved by rest/NTG. Unstable angina: Pain at rest, increasing frequency/severity (pre-MI).

questions.text: What is the normal range for hematocrit in adult males?
questions.explanation: Normal Hct: Males 42-52%, Females 37-47%. Hct is % of RBCs in blood. Low Hct indicates anemia; high Hct indicates polycythemia or dehydration.

questions.text: A client post-CABG (coronary artery bypass graft) should avoid:
questions.explanation: Post-CABG: Avoid driving 4-6 weeks (sternum healing). Do cough/deep breathe, use incentive spirometer. Splint chest with pillow. Gradual activity increase.

questions.text: What is a critical low platelet count?
questions.explanation: Critical thrombocytopenia: <20,000/mm³ (spontaneous bleeding risk). <50,000 = bleeding precautions. Avoid IM injections, rectal temps, aspirin.

title: Practice Test #1
description: Comprehensive Mixed Review - All NCLEX Categories

questions.text: A nurse is caring for four clients. Which client should be assessed first?
options: Client with diabetes, blood glucose 180 mg/dL
Client with COPD, respiratory rate 28, using accessory muscles
questions.explanation: Prioritize using ABCs. Respiratory distress (RR 28, accessory muscles) is life-threatening and requires immediate assessment. Other clients are stable.

questions.text: A client is receiving warfarin. Which food should the nurse teach the client to eat consistently?
questions.explanation: Leafy greens contain vitamin K which affects warfarin. Eat consistently (not avoid) to maintain stable INR. Avoid drastic changes in vitamin K intake.

questions.text: A 2-year-old is hospitalized. Which toy is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Toddlers (1-3 years): Parallel play, large toys (choking hazard with small pieces). Building blocks promote development. Puzzles/board games for older children.

questions.text: A client with schizophrenia says 'The FBI is watching me through the TV.' What is the best response?
options: 'That's not true, the FBI isn't watching you.'
'I don't see anyone watching, but I understand you feel frightened.'
'Let me turn off the TV for you.'
questions.explanation: Acknowledge feelings without reinforcing delusion. Don't argue or agree with delusion. Provide reality orientation while showing empathy.

questions.text: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin. Which action demonstrates proper technique?
questions.explanation: Rotate injection sites to prevent lipodystrophy. Don't massage (affects absorption), don't aspirate (not needed for SubQ), use abdomen/thigh (not deltoid for insulin).

questions.text: A client post-thyroidectomy develops numbness/tingling around mouth. What should the nurse do first?
questions.explanation: Paresthesias suggest hypocalcemia (parathyroid damage during surgery). Check Trousseau's/Chvostek's, notify provider, prepare calcium gluconate IV.

questions.text: A nurse is delegating tasks. Which can be delegated to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
options: Measuring vital signs on stable client
questions.explanation: UAP can do: vital signs, ADLs, ambulation, I&O on stable clients. Cannot: assess, teach, give meds, care for unstable clients. Nurses retain accountability.

questions.text: A pregnant client at 38 weeks has contractions every 3 minutes lasting 60 seconds. Cervix is 6 cm dilated. This is which stage of labor?
questions.explanation: First stage active phase: 4-7 cm dilation, contractions every 2-5 min. Latent: 0-3 cm. Second: 10 cm to delivery. Third: delivery to placenta.

questions.text: A client with heart failure has gained 5 pounds in 2 days. What is the priority intervention?
questions.explanation: Rapid weight gain (5 lbs = 2.3 kg = ~2.3 L fluid) indicates fluid retention. Restrict sodium/fluids, give diuretics, monitor I&O, daily weights.

questions.text: A child with epiglottitis is in the emergency department. What is the priority?
options: Keep child calm, prepare for intubation
questions.explanation: Epiglottitis: Airway emergency. Keep calm (crying worsens), upright position, don't examine throat (can cause obstruction), prepare for intubation. Give antibiotics.

questions.text: A client is taking phenytoin (Dilantin). Which finding should the nurse report?
questions.explanation: Ataxia and nystagmus indicate phenytoin toxicity. Report immediately, check drug level. Gingival hyperplasia is common side effect (good oral care). Drowsiness/nausea are mild.

questions.text: A client with pneumonia has a productive cough. Which position promotes drainage?
questions.explanation: Postural drainage uses gravity to drain secretions from specific lung segments. Position depends on affected area. Also: percussion, vibration, cough/deep breathe.

questions.text: A nurse witnesses a client fall. What should be documented?
options: 'Client found on floor, states slipped on water'
questions.explanation: Document objective facts and client statements. Avoid assumptions, blame, or judgmental language. Include assessment, interventions, notifications.

questions.text: A client with cirrhosis has asterixis. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Asterixis (flapping tremor) indicates hepatic encephalopathy from elevated ammonia. Give lactulose, restrict protein, monitor mental status.

questions.text: A client is prescribed furosemide. Which lab value should the nurse monitor?
questions.explanation: Loop diuretics (furosemide) cause potassium loss. Monitor for hypokalemia. Also lose calcium, magnesium. Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone) retain potassium.

questions.text: A newborn has Apgar score of 4 at 1 minute. What is the priority?
questions.explanation: Apgar 0-3 = severe distress, 4-6 = moderate distress (needs intervention), 7-10 = good. Score 4: Begin resuscitation (stimulation, oxygen, possible PPV).

questions.text: A client with tuberculosis is on isoniazid. Which supplement should be given?
questions.explanation: Isoniazid depletes vitamin B6, causing peripheral neuropathy. Give pyridoxine supplement. Also monitor liver function (hepatotoxicity risk).

questions.text: A client with myasthenia gravis is experiencing muscle weakness. Which medication should be available?
questions.explanation: Edrophonium (Tensilon test) differentiates myasthenic crisis (needs more anticholinesterase) from cholinergic crisis (too much medication). Have atropine ready for cholinergic crisis.

questions.text: A client post-mastectomy should avoid which activity on the affected side?
questions.explanation: Post-mastectomy: No BP, IV, injections, or blood draws on affected side (lymphedema risk). Do: ROM exercises, elevate arm, wear loose clothing, monitor for infection.

questions.text: A nurse is preparing to administer blood. Which action is correct?
options: Use 18-gauge needle, verify with two nurses, start within 30 minutes
Use 22-gauge needle, verify with one nurse, start within 2 hours
Warm blood in microwave before administration
questions.explanation: Blood administration: 18-20 gauge needle, two-nurse verification, start within 30 min of receiving, infuse over 2-4 hours max. Never microwave. Monitor for reactions.
